meaning: is used whenever the exact words of messages, letters, utterances are quoted; e.g. rəʾi-va zanta-ʾa qōbʾa-ʾa 'see this headband!', occasionally it is used in a narration (see also Bezold, ZA 15[1900].398). it is also suffixed to the exclamation particle ʾo; e.g. ʾoʾa gʾəyyu 'ho, ho, flee!' (see Dillmann 1907:382-3).
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. ʾi, also ʾay, as in ʾəndäy 'while not' (from ʾəndō-ay), Tna. ʾay (possibly from ʾal), Amh. yä (< ʾi) in yälläm 'there is not' (against allä 'there is'), Gur. e in e-š 'no!'. Amharic (ʾi)- to render 'in-' as in ʾi-säbʾawi 'in-human' is taken from Geez.

meaning: negative particle prefixed to the verb; e.g. ʾinəkl ḥawira 'we cannot go'; for emphasis it is also prefixed to the noun: ʾisamäya yaʾarra-gu 'they do not ascend to heaven'; the particle ʾi- can be repeated with the verb and with the noun for emphasis; e.g. 'I will scatter them among the nations ḥaba ʾiyyaʾam-məru ʾəmmuntu wäʾibawihomu (to those) whom neither they nor their fathers knew (know)'; the negative particle ʾi- is also used with the negative ʾalbo; e.g. wäʾimäy ʾalbo läʾlehu 'and there is no water above it'; when prefixed to the jussive it serves for the expression of prohibition; e.g. ʾitəmanu 'be-lieve not!'; combined with nouns it can correspond to the prefix 'un-, dis-', as in ʾimʾamäni 'disbeliever'; or it gives the noun a contradictory meaning, as in ʾiyäʾaməro 'ignorance', ʾisämi 'disobedience'; an interrogative particle with the negative ele-ment ʾi- becomes a negative indefinite: ʾi-man-nu, ʾi-mannu-hi 'no one, nobody'; ʾi-mənt, ʾi-məntə-hi, ʾi-məntə-ni 'nothing'; ʾi-ʾayte 'nowhere'; the element ʾi- is repeated in ʾi--wäʾi, wäʾi--wäʾi, ʾi.hi--wäʾi...hi, ʾi.ni--wäʾi...ni 'neither ...nor'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. ʾē, Heb. ʾī, Ph. ʾy, Ug. ʾiy, Akk. ay, ē.

meaning: interjection expressing pain, threat, admiration, wonder; as vocative it is either prefixed or suffixed (as -o) to the noun; e.g. ʾo-gabr 'o servant!', ʾo ʾabd 'thou fool!', bəʾəsit-o 'o woman!', ʾo-ke 'o what!'; occasionally prefixed and suffixed; e.g. ʾo-bəʾəsi-t-o 'o woman!' (Dillmann 1907:319-20); note that the vocative can be expressed without ʾo; e.g. ʾaḥazzən bəʾəntiʾäkki ʾiyərusälem 'I am sad for thy sake, o Jerusalem'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: for the expression of the vocative in the other Semitic languages, see Brockelmann 1913:33-5.

meaning: (suffixed to the noun), Tna. ʾo, Amh.Har. -o, hoy, Gaf. hoyä; also in Cushitic: Som. -ō. See also ʾoʾo.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. wo- (prefixed to the noun), -o
see ʾəlaw, below.

meaning: (Gr.247,T.M), ʾəʾenäʾon ʾəʾenäwən (K*) 'perpetual, eternal, who (that) lasts, who does not change, (T.M) being (n) eternal, (K) forever';
origin_explanation: from Gk. aiōnion, acc. sg. masc. of aiōnios 'per-petual, eternal'; also in Amh. ʾəʾenäwən 'eternal' (from Geez).
since the noun can be taken only from Tayyä even though M does not say so, it is undoubtedly a misprint for `watrikon`, below.
see also `atrikon`, `watrikon`.
SEMITIC: Ar. `a'ayā`, `hayyā` 'come on!'.
probably for `w'adal`, and see `adl`, below.
see (`anaga`), `'ang`.

meaning: 'father, forefather, ancestor, propagator, possessor or owner of (e.g. `aba lähm` 'owner of cattle'), abbot'
origin_explanation: the suffix pronouns are added to the base `abu-`, acc. `abä`
(written in T `abadam`)

SEMITIC: Ar. `ab` 'father', SAr. `b`, Mh. `hayb`, Heb. `āb`, Aram.-Syr. `abā`, Ug. `ab`, Akk. `abu`. ETHIOPIC: Te. `Tna. 'ab` 'father', Amh. `abbat` 'ab 'elder, forefather', Gaf. `ab'ä` 'father', Har. `āw`, Gur. `ab`; also in Cushitic: Sa.Af.Bi.Kham. `abba`, Som. `äba`. See also `abbā`.
from Heb. `ab`.

meaning: 'father!', an honorific title applied to religious leaders (bishop, patriarch, monk, abbot)
origin_explanation: from Aram.-Syr. `abbā`; also in Amh. `abba`. See `ab`.
pl. of `ubāl`, below.

meaning: 'servant who receives a wage paid in money or in a stipulated amount of grain'
origin_explanation: (Amh. `bändänna`)
probably from Amh. `abäba` 'flower'. Or could it be borrowed from Cushitic: Qem. `abāb` 'to pefume milk jar with the smoke of sweet-smelling herbs'?
SEMITIC: Ar. `ubāb` 'flow, wave', but is perhaps borrowed from Ar. `ubāb`.

meaning: name of the eleventh Coptic month
origin_explanation: (it corresponds to G. hamle) from Coptic (Bohairic) `epēp`, through Chr.-Ar. `abīb`. See also `appif`.
perhaps a variant of `ibn 'ibis'`, or of `eppoppa`.

meaning: 'be insane, become enraged, rage, be mad, be out of one's mind, become a fool, be foolish'
origin_explanation: (Dillmann 760 also translates `abda` 'run away' on the basis of 1 Kings 25:10, but the Asmara Bible edition has `tahat'a`) `a'abada`, causative; also 'lead away, seduce' `ta'abada` (Lt) 'be seduced, be led astray' `'asta'abada`, `'asta'abada` 'infuriate, treat as foolish, despise as a fool, ridicule, act mad' `abādī` 'ignorant, stupid, mad' `abūd` 'foolish, stupid, mad, insane, enraged, furious' `'abād` 'folly, foolishness, madness, insenselessness, being (n) out of one's senses' `abädāt` (pl.) 'foolish things' `'abd` (fem. `abadd`) 'fool, foolish, stupid, senseless, ignorant, imprudent, mad, insane' `mä'abadi` 'one who makes another be a deceiver, impostor, cheat' `masta'abad`, act. part. of `'asta'abada`; also 'scornful, buffoon' `'abadbid`, see below SEMITIC: Ar. `abada` 'become wild (cattle), away', Heb. `'abad` 'go astray, perish', Aram. `'abed` 'be lost', Syr. `'ebad` 'come to naught, be lost', `'abd` 'go astray, perish', Ug. `bd` 'perish', Akk. `'destroy'`. The basic meaning is 'be lost, go astray' either by not finding the way or in one's mind (become mad, wild), disappear, perish, be destroyed'. See also E. Jenni in Baumgartner 1967:148, n.7; Rabin in Ben-Hayyim 484. ETHIOPIC: Te. `abbāda` 'deceive', `'abd` 'fool'. Tna. `abbādä` 'entice with promises', `'abädä` 'become insane', Amh. `abbädä` 'be insane, go mad', passed into Cushitic: Sa. `abad`, `'abad` 'be silly, be foolish', Bil `'awad`. See also `harbada`.

from Cushitic: Bil. `abdä` 'eine Baumsorte deren Holz wegen des angenehmen Geruches zum Räuchern im Rauchbad der Frauen verwendet wird' (for which see Reinisch 1887:7), Reinisch, ib. compares Bil. `abdä` with Te. `'adba`.
`abadbidāt` (T.M) 'foolishness, stupidity, debility'; from `bd` with the reduplication of the last two radicals; see `abda`.
in connection with `'abadon`, below.
from Heb. `abaddon` 'ruin, perdition' through the Greek transcription `abaddōn`. See also `'abadon`, above.

composed of `abbä` `g'änbäh` (`gunbäh`); SEMITIC: Dillmann 1176 quoting Ludolf compares it with Ar. `gunbuh` 'fat, stout, tall'. ETHIOPIC: Te. `abbä` `genbah`, Tna. `abbägunbah`, `abbagumbah`; in Reinisch 1887:151 `abbäg` `em-ba`, `abbäg` `enbäht`.
see bwh II, `bəha`.

meaning: 'flesh, piece of flesh, member of body, member (of a community), limb, genitals, self, person'
origin_explanation: `abāla zar` 'genitals' (for the euphemism, cp. Heb. `'eber` 'member of body, genitalia'); ETHIOPIC: Te. `habal` 'sexual organ', Tna. `abal` 'body', Amh. `gur. abal` 'body, member of the body, sexual organ'. The suggestion made by Praetorius 1879:17 (and accepted by Wajnberg 1935:15) to connect Amh. `ballät` 'part of the body' with `abāl` (Amh. `abal`) is not convincing.
could it be the a-stem of `bəhla` 'say' with a meaning corresponding to Amh. `asännä` 'name, designate'? It could also be connected with Amh. `abäll` 'allowance, allotment'.

meaning: 'a hardwood tree growing in the highland'
origin_explanation: Amh. `yäddäga abälo` 'a hardwood tree growing in the highland'.
from Gk. `obolos` 'a coin'; also Amh. `aboli`. See also `wabale`.

meaning: one of the names of the moon; a corrupt form of Heb. ləḇānā 'moon' (so Halévy, JA 9[1867]385).
origin_explanation: a corrupt form of Heb. ləḇānā 'moon' (so Halévy, JA 9[1867]385).
lit. 'our father'; see ab.

meaning: epact (the difference in days between a solar year and a lunar year), the number of days since the new moon at the beginning of the year, adjustment or correction made in a year to keep the calendar in step with the solar year'; (abäbä warh [Lt] 'lunar year').
origin_explanation: from Gk. epaktē 'intercalary day' through Chr.-Ar. abäbä, also in Tna. abäbä, Amh. abäbä. See also abäbä. On the epact, see Chaine 104-6; Neugebauer 188 ff.
(K considers abär a plural of abər); SEMITIC: Ar. 'ibra 'needle'.

meaning: old woman, widow, unmarried woman, (Lt) old man'; abäräwit, abäräy (fem. abäräwit, abäräy) 'old man, old woman'; abret (Gr.241) 'old woman, nun'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. abreh 'old age'. There is no connection between G. abär and Ar. armala 'widow', as suggested by Brockelmann 1908:227. Likewise unlikely is the connection with Heb. abbîr 'strong', Akk. abru suggested by Baumgartner 9 since one would not expect to connect old age with strength. ETHIOPIC: Te. abbära 'become old', 'aber 'old', barä 'be old'. Gur. aber 'grandfather, grandmother'. The connection with Te. abär 'lack, defect' suggested by Littmann-Höfner 366 is doubtful. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Sa. bara 'old man', Or. bera 'old woman', Had. ebära 'old'. Som. habar 'old woman'. Praetorius, ZDMG 47 (1893), 392 thinks that the Semitic-Ethiopic root is taken from Cushitic.

meaning: branches of a chandelier'; pl. of bär, below.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. abra, same (from Geez).
from Amh. abärbärä; perhaps borrowed from Cushitic: Qem. abärbärä.

meaning: The game called gäbäṭa in Amharic.

origin_explanation: from Gk. praxeis 'Acts (of the Apostles)' through Chr.-Ar. abrakis.

phonetics: abrim (T) abrim (Gr.435) 'Pater in divinis, the Father, (T) heart'.
meaning: 'heart' could be a transferred meaning of 'Father'. See also abrawə, aberyon.
origin_explanation: 'heart' could be a transferred meaning of 'Father'. See also abrawə, aberyon.
see bry II.
see bry II.

meaning: priest who acts on behalf of the bishop'; from Gk. epitropos 'man in charge' through Chr.-Ar. abrūṭus.
origin_explanation: from Gk. epitropos 'man in charge' through Chr.-Ar. abrūṭus.
see abrotros, above.
is either a variant or a misprint of abrim, above.
see also abrim.
from Gk. abbās, of Aramaic (vabbā) origin.

meaning: sin, transgress, do wrong, act wrongly, commit a crime, be guilty, offend'; abbäsa, causative; abbäsi (pl. abbäsäyän), act. part. of abbäsa; abbus 'wicked, criminal, who acts wrongly, sinner, slippery'; abäsä (pl. abäsät) 'transgression, serious fault, iniquity, offense, sin, crime, guilt'; absat 'act of sinning, transgressing, trespass, wickedness'; maabbäs 'sinner, wrongdoer, criminal, transgressor, guilty'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. abasa 'receive badly, despise, offend'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. abbäsa 'act wrongly'. Te. abbäsa 'sin'. Har. abbäsa 'trouble, misfortune'. Amh. abbäsa

meaning: gulley, watercourse formed by erosion, bank (of a river)'; from Gk. Oubal, proper name of a river, transcription of Heb. 'ûḇāl 'stream'. See also abul.
origin_explanation: from Gk. Oubal, proper name of a river, transcription of Heb. 'ûḇāl 'stream'. See also abul.

meaning: hill'; probably a misunderstanding of ubäl I.
origin_explanation: probably a misunderstanding of ubäl I.
variant of absalates, below.
is borrowed from Cushitic: Qem. abläliṭ. Note that Amh. abläliṭ could also be a variant of aläbläliṭ 'nettle', from läblälabä 'burn'.
from Ar. iblīs (for its derivation from Gk. diabolos, see Jeffery 47); also in Te. ablis.

meaning: shimmering of a gridle'; variant of appolitu, below.
origin_explanation: variant of appolitu, below.

phonetics: əbn I (pl. aban, aban, abänät) əbn
meaning: stone'; often combined with names of precious stones, as in abna särdäyon, abna sanper, abna admas, and others; (abna barad 'hailstone'); (abna faws 'eye salve'); (abna ashätäyä 'crystal'); (zaabn 'stony'); abana 'become a stone' (denominative); abana 'change into a stone'; taabna (Lt) 'become a stone'; abun (Gr) 'hard like a stone'; abnäwi (Gr) 'pertaining to a stone'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. aban, proper name of Petra, 'stone', Soq. oben, Heb. eben, Aram.-Syr. Md. abna, Ph. bn, Ug. abn, Akk. abnu. ETHIOPIC: Te. abən, Tna. əmni (with alternate labials), Gur. aman, Har. ün (with b > b > zero), Amh. abnä bärädo 'marble', Tna. əmnä bärädo.
from Ar. ()ibn.
from Gk. ibin, acc. sg. of ibis 'ibis'; see also yəbn.

from Ar. abnūs; also Syr. abnūsā, Amh. abnūs. As Gk. ebenos 'ebony', it is considered to be of Semitic origin (see Baumgartner 227, under hbn). For Heb. hobnîm, of Egyptian origin, see Lambdin 149; cp. also Ug. hbn.
from Amh. abənnät; also Tna. abənnät.
from Gk. apokalypsis 'Apocalypse' through Chr.-Ar. abūkalamšis, abūqalamšis; also in Te. Tna. abūqalamšis, Amh. abūqalamšis.

meaning: a bird that devours snakes'; corrupt form of alyäṭon, below.
origin_explanation: corrupt form of alyäṭon, below.

meaning: dog that has little ones'; see also əlbäzər.
origin_explanation: see also əlbäzər.

meaning: kind of fragrant spice, cinnamon, (Gr.241) iris, (T) a plant whose bark is used for fumigating people or cattle ill with typhus' (in Amh. qäbäräččo); from Gk. amōmon 'spice-plant' (see Brockelmann 1908:226, with m-m > b-m).
origin_explanation: from Gk. amōmon 'spice-plant' (see Brockelmann 1908:226, with m-m > b-m).
transcription of Heb. 'ābāš 'fatten'. Note, however, Amh. abbāšā 'grease, oil' which could possibly indi cate an Ethiopic cognate with Heb. 'bš.
see also 'abus qubus, 'eppis qoppos.
see 'ebis kobos, and 'eppis qoppos.
variant of 'absalātes, below.
from Gk. psaltēs 'cantor' through Chr.-Ar. absalt. See also 'absalidis, above, 'abfals, and pasalte, below.
from Latin absolutus 'absolved'.
perhaps from Gk. *eu syndetikos 'well binding to gether' (W. Müller).
corrupt from Gk. ibis 'ibis' (Hommel 1877:XXIX).
from Lat. amictorium 'garment' which is thrown over one, 'wrap', through Gk., or Syr.-Aram. miqtōrīn.
misprint for 'asata hlm.
Perhaps from Gk. epimelētēs 'curator, administra tor' (Müller).

meaning: (T) 'decree'; 'Orders of the Apostles' (a collection of canon laws)
origin_explanation: misreading of Ar. 'al-ta'līfāt (from Gk. titlos 'inscription, decree', from Latin titulus) as 'al-ta'līfāt 'al-battālīsāt confusing t and b. See also 'absaltis, *taqlis.
see 'absalātes.
could it be compared with Amh. abbay 'Blue Nile'? However, abbay undoubtedly means originally 'the big one' from the root 'by (for which see 'abaya).

meaning: (Y) 'refuse, be unwilling (to do), revolt, disobey, decline, say no, oppose, resist, reject, deny, disagree'
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC: Ar. 'abā ('by) 'refuse'. On Heb. 'ābā 'want, desire' as well as on this root meaning 'want, desire' in some Arabic dialects, see Landberg 1920. On the original meaning of 'by 'want, desire', see Noeldeke 1904:66. Delitzsch 11 gives the root basic meaning 'decide', hence 'agree' or 're fuse'. Baumgartner 3 suggests the basic meaning 'mangeln, bedürftig sein'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'abāyā 'refuse', Te. 'aba, Amh. ab bāyā 'disobey', 'abaya 'refractory ox', Gur. 'ebba 'disobey'.

meaning: 'hand, arm, handle, haft, part, side, place, (Lt. (transferred meaning) favor, power, -fold'; the suffix pronouns are attached to the bade 'ade-, 'ədā 'where, wherever'; ba-'ədā- 'by, through, by the inter mediary, thanks to'
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC: Ar. yad 'hand', SAr. yd, Soq. yd, Aram. yadā, Syr. 'īdā, Md. 'dā 'hand', 'through', Ug. Ph. yd 'hand', Akk. idu. A possible root 'd in Hebrew and Ugaritic, see Dahood 1966:88, 95. For a Heb. root d, see JJS 6 (1955). 112; for 'd in Arabic dialects, see Rabin ib. 84, 92. For this biradical root, see Noel deke 1910:113-6. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'ade, Tna. 'id, Amh. 'əgg, Gur. 'āg, Arg. 'ang.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. addāfā 'be dirty', Arg. 'adafā also in Cushitic: Kham. edif 'filth', Qem. 'ədəf 'dirty'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. adago 'low-lying hot country'.

meaning: 'ass'; 'anast 'she-ass'; 'gadām 'wild donkey' (see gadām); 'marab 'pelican, wild ass' (see marab); 'millstone' (see haradā)
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC: Barth 1893:45 connects it with Ar. 'ilg 'wild ass' (with alternance l:d, an alternance that is not regular). M. Cohen, GLECS 6 (1951-4). 16 identifies it with Syr. dūgā' 'deaf' (with the passage of meaning to 'be dumb'), and adeg 'become deaf'. However the Syriac root is dgg and 'adeg is an af'el of dgg. J. Halevy, MSL 13 (1905-6). 65 derives it from Heb. 'qd, 'qd 'tie, bind' and states that 'l'âne est rarement débarassé de son bât et des sangles qui l'attachent au dos'. None of the etymologies ed by the authors mentioned above is convincing.

meaning: ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'adami 'donkey', Te. 'adg 'adama gadām, cp. Tna. 'adgi bārākā 'wild ass', Te. 'adg kādān.
(Gr.250) 'garment, vestment (seems to be of leather), shirt, tunic, cloak, underwear'
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC: perhaps Akk. adīlu 'a garment for festivi ties' (D. Cohen 1970:9). ETHIOPIC: Amh. 'adāl 'skin, sackcloth' (from Geez). See also 'adal.

SEMİTİC: Ar. 'ādamiyy 'human' (from the proper name Adam), Ar. (Hadr) 'awādim (pl. of 'ādam) 'people', SAr. 'dm 'vassals, subjects', Heb. 'ādām 'man, mankind', Ph. 'dm, Ug. 'adm 'mankind'. For a bibliography on this root, see D. Cohen 1970:9. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'addam 'men, people'.

meaning: 'be agreeable, be pleasing, be pleasant, please, be beautiful, seem best, seem good, be subtle'; with object suffix pronouns 'be pleased, take delight, enjoy' (e.g. 'addamo 'he was pleased', lit. 'it pleased him'); 'addama (Lt) 'find something pleasant'; ta'addama (Lt) 'be pleased with, delight in'; 'asta'addama 'have pleasure, be pleased with, consider pleasing, find pleasing, delight in, be come desirous of'; 'asta'addama (Lt) 'find agreeable'; 'addām 'pleasant, agreeable, fair, de lightful, charming, beautiful'; ('addāma 'with pleasure, agreeably'); 'addum 'pleasing, agreeable'; 'addōmo 'delight'; ma'addām 'pleasing, pleasant, charming, hand some'; tə'addəmt (Lt) 'pleasure, enjoyment, delight'.
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC: the Geez root 'dm 'be pleasant' goes back to the meaning 'red' (so Dillmann 800 following Gesenius; also Ullendorff 1968:126). For 'red', see 'awādamā, below. For Heb. 'ādōm 'also pleasant, delicious', see Eitan 28; Ullendorff, ib. The deriva tion of 'addama from *'ad'ama and its identification with n'm (with alternance d:n) is suggested by Praeto rius 1890:46-7 is unlikely.
(from Geez) passed into Cushitic: Bil. adam 'be pleasant'.

from Ar. adim 'hide, skin, leather' (possibly from the red or brown color; see addämäwi); also in Te.Tna. adim 'leather', Amh. adim 'red leather', Har. adim 'tanned hide (mostly red)'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. adim 'hide of brownish color', Som. idin.
perhaps from adim 'red or brown color'.
from Heb. eden 'garden of Paradise' through Gk. edom; also Amh. edom 'Eden, garden of Paradise'.

from Gk. adamas 'adamant, diamond'; also Syr. adamas, adamwas, Md. dmasa 'diamond'; in Ethiopic: Tna. admas 'diamond', Amh. admas 'plumb bob', abnä admas 'diamond'; corrupted in Ar. al-mas 'diamond' and borrowed by Te. almas, Amh. almas, almaz.

SEMITIC: Ar. adima 'be brown', Heb. adam 'be red' (for Heb. adom 'pleasant', see addama, above), Ug. dm 'rouge oneself', Akk. adamu 'red'. On a possible connection between dm 'be red' and dam 'blood' (cp. also Aram. adam 'blood'), see Buhl 10, D. Cohen 1970:9. ETHIOPIC: Amh. addämä 'be blood-red' (probably derived from adim 'red leather', for which see above).
from Heb. adonäy 'God'.
from Heb. adar; also Amh. adar (from Geez).

meaning: (K) 'the fourth nail with which Christ was crucified';

origin_explanation: transcription of Heb. adrammelek, name of an idol; in Syr. adramelk.

meaning: (K*) 'myrtle, plant which has fragrant leaves that are used for perfuming butter to be put on the hair';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hadas 'myrtle', Heb. hadas, hadassa. ETHIOPIC: Tna. adas, same, Amh. adas (for adas see Strelcyn 1973:no. 7).

meaning: 'sweep'; tadawa (K*) 'be swept'; adäwi (K*), act. part. of adawa; addäw 'swept, clean'; addäw 'sweepings, filth, rubbish, fuse';
pl. of addäw (Lt) 'dirt'; maddäw 'broom, (T.M) shovel, container for rubbish';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the connection with Ar. idäwa 'receptacle for holding water for ablutions' suggested by KEL 171 (who translates Ar. idäwa 'Waschbecken') and G. adawa 'sweep' being a denominative of addäw 'sweepings' is not convincing. ETHIOPIC: Gur. aggä 'sweep' (Polotsky, JAOS 69[1949].39), Har. adu 'manure'.
see asara 'squeeze'.

meaning: (pl. afaw, afawät) 'mouth, entrance, brim, orifice, border, edge (of a sword)';
origin_explanation: the suffix pronouns are attached to the base af (acc. (with suffix pronouns) afä- (zakolæ afu-hu 'two-edged'); af baf 'mouth to mouth, face to face'; afä säbäh 'dawn, morning'; afä nagh 'dawn'; ba-afä hassin, ba-afä sayf 'at the point of the sword'). SEMITIC: Ar. fü (in construct state) 'mouth', 'voice, mouth', Heb. pe, Ph.Ug. p, Akk. pi, final -m, in Ar. fam, Aram.-Syr. pummä, Md. pum. On the root see Noeldeke 1910:171-8, and Gartner 864. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. af, Amh.Arg.Har.Gur. af in Cushitic: Som.Sa.Af.Qua. af 'mouth', Kam.Qab. afu (see also Dolgopolsky 231 1947:no. 380 bis). See also afawa.
ETHIOPIC: Te. hafa 'sharp curved knife', Amh.afa 'short sword'. See also afot, hafä.
from Heb. epä through Greek transcription oiphi. Also Te. uf. Note that Heb. epä is considered to be of Egyptian (ipt) origin (Sethe, ZAS 62[1927].61).

meaning: 'how?, how about?, in what way?, why?, how!, how great!, what!'; affo-nu, affo-nu-ma 'how?'; ba-affo 'in what way?, why?'; affo za-, ba-affo za-, affo kama, ba-affo kama 'for what?, and why?'; affo-ke 'how much more?, how much less?' (depending on the context); zakama affo 'of what sort?, how much, in what manner?, how?'; affo anka, affo angä, affo-nu-ma anka, affo fadfada, affo-ke fadfada, affo anka 'how much more?'; affoffo, affäffo, affäffo, affä-affo 'how?'; affoyäwi (T) 'such a what a!';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. if-ol 'how?' (Leslau 1938:69), Heb. epo 'how?' (in Judges 8:18; see Buhl 32). On the particle pa in Semitic, see G. Garbini, Biblica 38 (1957).419-27; and AION 21 (1971).245-8. reduplication of the last radical in affoffo, etc. afkaka 'how?'. The element f also occurs in kayfa 'how?', but its identification with G. af suggested by Jensen 1887:423, is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te. afo 'how?'.

meaning: 'out, outside, outside part, outdoors, outward, outside of, on the outside, beyond'; with suffix pronouns: e.g. ba-afä-hu 'outside of it'; preceded or followed by prepositions: ba-afä 'outside, outwardly', anta afä 'abroad, outdoors', amanna afä, amanna afä 'outside of, from without', aska afä 'out, outdoors', afä 'am, afä amanna 'outside of' (za-afä 'outer, external'); (sab`a afä 'outsiders'); afäwi 'external, outward, outer, outsider (in religion)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. afä 'outside', Amh. afä (from Geez); also in Cushitic: Be. efo 'front part of the house', Aw. af 'outside, outdoors'.

SEMITIC: Ar. af`a 'viper', Heb. epe(h), Ug. p` 'reptile' (Aistleitner, Caquot 442). See also fa`awa. ETHIOPIC: Te. afot 'viper', Tna. af`ut, Amh. afot, Har. amfut, Gur. afä`a; related to Amh. affunnat, Har. hiffin, Gur. umfänne, passed into Cushitic: Bil. af`ota.

meaning: 'the upper garment of the priest of Israel in biblical times';
origin_explanation: from Heb. epod, same, through Gk. ephoud; also in Amh. efud. In the other Semitic languages: Syr. apudä, Pedä, Ug. ipd, Akk. epattu, pl. epadätu 'a costly garment' (see Baumgartner 75; D. Cohen 1970:28).
SEMITIC: the comparison with hpk 'turn over' (Heb. hapak, and others) suggested by Dillmann 808 (followed by D. Cohen 1970:28) is not convincing.
(D 1406), aliflusäwiyän. from Gk. pyloros 'gate keeper'. See also arlefalus.
see asfälito, below.
perhaps from Gk. `eupnoos` 'sweet-smelling' (Müller).

phonetics: afqud (T) 'female goat, sheep which has not yet been covered'
meaning: 'female goat, sheep which has not yet been covered'
origin_explanation: it looks like a plural of `faqd`. It is either a misprint of `afqus` (see below) or the other way around.

phonetics: (T) 'sheep or goat which has not yet been covered'
meaning: 'sheep or goat which has not yet been covered'

phonetics: (pl. 'afawāt, 'afāwāt) (H) 'sweet odor, scent, fragrance, perfume'; 'afāwāt' 'sweet herbs, perfumed unguents, spices'
meaning: 'sweet odor, scent, fragrance, perfume'; 'sweet herbs, perfumed unguents, spices'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. `'afwāh` 'aromatics', from Geez (G. Hoff-mann, ZDMG 32[1878].761) with a sg. `fūh` recon-structed from the plural (Noeldeke 1910:237-8). ETHIOPIC: The root also occurs in Cushitic: `abbuwā` 'pleasant odor, aroma, perfume'. Be. `'odōr` 'fūmigate or aroma'. Ab. `'abāb` 'fūmigate a utensil for milk'. see Conti Rossini 1912:158.

SEMITIC: Ar. `'afr, 'afar` 'dust', Heb. `'āpār, 'eper`. ETHIOPIC: Amh.Arg.Har.Gur. `'afār` 'earth, soil', Gaf. `'afārā`. Dillmann 808 considers G. `'afar` an Amharic loanword, unless it is to be identified with Heb. `'eper`, for which see Kutscher, Leshonenu 27-8 (1964). 184-5.
denominative from `'af` 'mouth'.
SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) `hafā (hfy)` 'bake', `mifd` 'oven'. SAr. `fy` 'baked', Soq. `mofe` 'furnace', Heb. `'āpā ('py)` 'bake', Aram. `'āpe, Syr. 'epā, Md. `apa, Ug. 'py, Akk. `epū`.

phonetics: in beta `afsāt` (Lt), `beta` `afsot` (OT) 'hospice for lepers, (OT) who are by themselves, separated from others'
meaning: 'hospice for lepers, (OT) who are by themselves, separated from others'
origin_explanation: from Gk. `aphpousōth`, transcription of Heb. `bet hahōpšīt`.
SEMITIC: related to Ar. `'afā (fy)` 'be dense'.
is perhaps to be identified with `afsāt, afsot` above, that is, the storage for grain in a house by itself separated from the main house.
from Gk. `ophiomakhon`, acc.sg. of `ophiomakhos` 'kind of locust'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. `afa` 'short sword'.

phonetics: (Sw. in D 1406), 'efot (OT) 'small drum kept beside a large one used for summoning troops' (Amh. `ṭarān`).
meaning: 'small drum kept beside a large one used for summoning troops'

origin_explanation: it occurs in the expression `bāzganat za'efot` 'necklace of `'efot` (Judges 8:26). Neither the Hebrew text nor the Septuagint gives any indication about the mean-ing of `'efot`. Could it mean 'the fringe of the `'efot`' and `'efot` would then be a transcription of Heb. `'epōd`?
from Amh. `aggādā`, same.

phonetics: (H) 'thighbone, shinbone, tibia, leg, large bone of the leg, shoulder of animal'
meaning: 'thighbone, shinbone, tibia, leg, large bone of the leg, shoulder of animal'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. `agādā` 'leg (of man), hind leg (of cow), shinbone'; also in Cushitic: Af. `agedā` 'arm'. Sa. `agādā`.
ETHIOPIC: Te. `'agāle` 'so-and-so', Tna. `'agālā, 'akā`. Amh. `agāle`. Gur. `agāle`. Conti Rossini 1912: compares it with Qem. `ayla`, Khamta `aylāt`.

phonetics: (pl. 'ag 'al, 'ag 'alāt) (T) `ag` (K) `ag` (A) 'the young of any animal or fowl' (also of humans, but the more frequent term is `ag 'āl`, below); `ag` 'alt', `ag` 'elt' (pl. 'ag 'alāt, 'ag 'āl)` 'calf, heifer'

origin_explanation: (faṭrata `ag 'āla 'amahāyāw` 'human creature') `agala`: one would expect `ag` (g)`ala`.

(M 394 misunderstood Amh. `hākārā` 'large shield' [Tayyā's translation of G. `'agre` for `bāk` r` and, therefore, wrongly renders G. `'agre` by 'firstling, first-born']; `aggārāwi` 'warrior'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. `agre` 'large shield', Har.Gur. `agri` from Cushitic: Had. `agira` (Cerulli 1936:231).

phonetics: (pl. 'agar, 'agār) (H) 'foot, measure'; (agra 'amir, 'agra dahāy` 'ray of sunlight, beams of the sun'); (ba-agr, 'anta 'agr` 'on foot'); (saba 'agr` 'infantry'); (ba'ahatti sa'āt 'agr` [Lt] 'at the first hour'); `aggārāri (pl. 'aggārārān, 'aggārāyān)` 'pedes-trian, infantry'; `aggārāwi` 'pedestrian'; `agrāwi (K*)` 'worm with many feet'
meaning: 'foot, measure'; 'ray of sunlight, beams of the sun'; 'on foot'; 'infantry'; 'at the first hour'; 'pedestrian, infantry'; 'pedestrian'; 'worm with many feet'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. `'agar`, Tna. `'agri`, Amh. `agar`, Har. `igir, ingir`, Gaf. `ag` 'rā`, Gur. `agār`, Arg. `ingir`. The comparison with Heb. `regel` 'foot', Ar. `riğl` in Buhl 744 and Koehler 872 on the authority of Brockel-mann 1908:227, 231, and Růžička 55 (so also Litt-mann-Höfner 386) is doubtful. For its doubtful connection with `'iğr, 'ağr` of the Arabic dialects of Datina and Syria, see Landberg 1920:63, Noeldeke ZA 20 (1907). 414, and Leslau 1958:49. For Syrian-Arabic `eger`, Barthélemy 3 suggests a derivation from `leğr` (by metathesis of `reğl`). I being taken for the article so that the form `eger` is taken for the noun. König 144 (on the authority of Ewald) derives `agr` from `'algar` (with prothetic `i`) > `lagar` < `ragal`.
perhaps from Cushitic: Sa. `egarin` 'orange'.
from Gk. `khoirogrōllion`, acc. of `khoirogrōllios` 'rock badger'.

meaning: apparently represents Gk. `gōniai` 'corners' which has no correspondent in the Hebrew text of Ez. 41:15 quoted by Dillmann.
origin_explanation: apparently represents Gk. `gōniai` 'corners' which has no correspondent in the Hebrew text of Ez. 41:15 quoted by Dillmann.
secondary form of `hagrata`.

From Gk. hagios 'holy', fem. hagiā; also Syr. yagyā. See yagyāsmānton, below.
From Gk. hagios 'holy', fem. hagiā; also Syr. yagyā. See yagyāsmānton, below.
corrupt. from Gk. hagiou pneumatos 'of the holy spirit', gen.sg. of hagion pneuma. See also yagyā, above.

meaning: lit. 'the master of the land' (see gaz'a I, baher); agzi'ab(a)hērāwi 'divine'; agzi'ab(a)hērannā 'divine nature of God';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. yagzi'abyaher, abbreviated (with metathesis) yagzi. Te. yagzaher, Amh. yagzi'abyaher, agzer, yagzi'iher.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. yagazān, yagāzān, Amh. agazān; from Cushitic: Aw. agāzeni, Qem. agāzēn.

meaning: (Lt) yah, exclamation of pain or grief, sighing; yah bahla (Lt) 'groan';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. yah, yāh, yāh, exclamation of pain, yāh 'moan, complain'. Heb. yāh yāh 'alas', related to yāh, yāh. Syr. yah. Note that in Heb. yahālay, yahāley 'alas' the preposition l'to, for' is agglutinated with yah (cp. G. way and wayle 'woe!'). ETHIOPIC: Te. yah yah 'oh!', Amh. ah, exclamation of sorrow, grief, yāh yāh 'utter a cry of sorrow', Gur. ux bara 'sigh'; also in Cushitic: Bil. aha, Sa.Af. ah, Som. oh.

meaning: (Huf) yū, expression of consent, 'certainly!, yes, indeed, by all means, all right, I am here!, (Lt) agreement, consent'; oho bahla (yabe) 'agree, consent, obey, promise, submit, be submissive'; oho yabala, causative; also 'persuade'; oho bahloť 'compliance, submissiveness, obedience'; (bahila oho [Lt] 'consent, agreement');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. yiy 'yes, certainly'. Hars. yahā 'yes', Ug. i 'truly'.
ETHIOPIC: Te. yaho, yuhu 'yes!', Tna. yaha 'all right', yaho in Cushitic: Bil. ahō 'all right, here I am!'. Dillmann 1907:114 analyzes it, with reservations, as yo (see y O, above) and -ho.
from Ar. hūt.

SEMITIC: Onomatopoetic, as in Ar. yahha 'cough', yah 'sound of coughing'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. yuhu bālā, yaha bālā 'cough', yuhu bela, Amh. yuhu ala, yahu ala, Har. oho Gur. uxu barā; also in Cushitic: Sa. oho, Or.
ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. yaha 'cattle'. Cohen 1947:11 identifies it with Egyptian yh 'ox'; also Coptic ehe 'cow'. Littmann, Deutsche Literaturzeitung 1931, p. 1404 suggests a common African origin.

meaning: (acc. yahada) yah (masc.) 'one certain, a, same, only one'; (yahadu yahadu, lalla yahadu yahadu, babba yahadu 'every one, each one, separately, individually'); (yi-yahadu 'not one'); (yi-ba-yahadu 'not in the slightest'); (yahadu...wayahadu 'one...and the other'); yahād (pl. yahādāt) 'unit (numbers from one to ten), unique'; yahuď 'first, first day of the week, Sunday'; yahuď sanbat 'Sunday, first day of the week'; yahada (K*) 'make one, be one' (denominative); tayahāda (K*) 'become united, be linked together'; tayahād 'unity, being united' (n); mayahād (M) 'one part';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. wahad 'one'. Sar. hd, Heb. yēhād, Aram.-Syr. had, Md. had, Ug. ahd, Ph. hd, Akk. wēdu 'only, alone, single'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hade 'one', Te. had 'one another (the expression for 'one' is woro). Gur. had, Amh. and, Arg. hand (for ad with inserted n), Gaf. yagā. For the feminine, see yahatti. The root is connected with yhd, below.

meaning: (MA) 'assemble in a row, arrange in order'.

origin_explanation: from Ar. ahmar.

ETHIOPIC: perhaps related to Te. waher 'bull'. The comparison with Amh. awra 'male' suggested by d'Abbadie 548 is doubtful. The root is common with Cushitic: Sa.Af. awur, aur 'bull', Som. yaur 'stallion', unless Cushitic: awur, aur is to be identified with Amh. awra. For Cushitic *awr-, see Sasse 46.

meaning: (for yahadti; acc. yahatta) yah (fem.) 'one, only one, same'; yahatta 'one time, once, one (thing), at the same time'; (yi-ba-yahatti 'by no means'); yahattane 'together, in one place'; (ba-yahattane, wasta yahattane, same);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. yihdā 'one' (fem), Sar. ht, Heb. yahāt, Aram. hādā, Syr. hodā, Md. hda, Ph. ht, Ug. aht. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hanti, Te. hatte, hante, Amh. and. For the masculine, see yahadu.

meaning: yahil 'heap (mass) of dried figs or dates, dough made from figs or dates, (T) date palm, dried figs'; see also helā II, helā.
yabe kept back, be delayed, (T.Y) make follow'; yahara 'keep back, delay'; yahāri 'hindmost, latter'; yahārit (M) 'extremity, end';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. yahhara 'delay', yāhir 'last'. Sar. h-hr 'delay', Sh. aheri 'the second', Soq. her-hen 'behind'. Heb. yahār 'behind', Aram. yahār 'behind', Syr. yahār 'after', Ug. hr 'remain after'. Ak. ahāru 'remain behind'. ETHIOPIC: Te. har 'behind' (Te yaker 'end' is an Arabic loanword). Har. hār-le 'behind', Gur. (Zway) ir-āni (or irā-ni). For Te. rāhar 'from behind, afterwards', Gur. (Wolane) rer 'back of the body', bā-rer 'after', see Leslau 1979:527.

meaning: 'when, at the time when'; am-uma, 'amənna 'ama 'from the time of, from the time when, from when'; 'am-uma 'as long as, until the time of, until the time that, until (prep., conj.); la-'ama 'till, until, at the time when (conj.), at the time of'; 'albo 'ama 'not a day (like this), there is not a time when, at no time, never'; 'albo-hu 'ama, 'albo-ni 'ama 'never'; bo-'ama 'sometimes'; bo-'ama---bo-'ama 'at one time---at another time'; za-'ama 'at the time (of this) that'; the suffix pronouns are attached to the base 'ame-; e.g. 'am-ame-ka 'from your time, from the time that you live', 'aska 'ame-hu 'until his time'; the most frequent form is 'ame-hu, 'ame-ha 'when, at that time, next'; za-'ame-hu, za-'ame-ha, '(the) then'; 'am-'ame-ha 'from then on, from that time on, thereafter'; note that 'ame also occurs without suffix pronouns; e.g. 'ame 'alata dayn 'at the day of judgment'.

Noeldeke 1910:135, n. 1 expresses doubts about G. 'am = yom. For 'day' coming to mean 'when', cp. also Soq. 'am 'when', Sh. yem. On yom used as a conjunction in various Arabic dialects, see Leslau 1939:112; also Neo-Syr. 'imā 'when'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. 'ama 'when' (from Geez).

Syr. amin 'true, lasting', ememen 'be steadfast, persevere', Md. amn 'be firm, be true'. See on this root in Semitic Hans Wildberger in Baumgartner 1967:372-386; Marrassigni 80-82. Aram. innin. For -t added to a plural form, cp. Sar.Ph. hm-t, Ar. (Spain) humat, A. gen. unuti. For -ntu in G. ammuntu, cp. also allun-tu (masc.), alläntu (fem.) 'these'. Eitan, AJSL 44 (1927-8): 255 analyzes G. ammuntu as coming from ammu-anta 'they who'. Littmann 1964:355 derives it from ammu-n-tu.

meaning: 'show, indicate, tell, make a sign, make known, demonstrate, inform, in-struct, refer'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. amara 'order, command', Sar. mr 'sign, oracle', Heb. amar 'say' (for Heb. amar 'see' and a bibliography on the subject, see Dahood 1970:62; for he'emir 'he recognized', see Ullendorff 1968:127), Aram. amar 'say, speak'. Syr. emar. Md. amr, Ph. mr, Ug. mr 'see', Akk. amaru 'see'. See also Marrassigni 106ff. Dillmann 728 seems to have been the first to state the original meaning of the root mr as 'stand out, be conspicuous, be clear, be bright > make clear, show, say'. These meanings of the root mr were confirmed subsequently by Delitzsch 28; Moscati, Biblica 27 (1946):115-26; Al-bright, JAOS 74 (1954), 229, n. 7 (who claims that the original meaning was 'to see' and that the shift in meaning came through the factitive stem 'to show', hence 'to speak'), and Rundgren, Orientalia 32 (1963):181-3. For a more detailed bibliography see Marrassigni 110; F.A.Dombrowski, ZDMG 134 (1984), 37, n. 41. ETHIOPIC: Te. amara 'know, understand', ammara 'be bright, be clear', Tna. ammara 'show', Amh. ammara 'show, indicate'. For amart 'miracle, won-der', cp. Tna. amarti, Te. amara, Amh. ammar (from Geez).
from Ar. amir; also in Amh. amer.

meaning: sun, day, time; also 'the first day of the month' (e.g. 'amiru wa'amiru 'the eleventh day of the month', lit. 'the tenth day and the first day'); k"ello 'amira 'all day', lalla 'amiru 'every day', wa'otta 'amira 'on that day';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Gaf. aymora 'sun', Gurage imir, yimar 'sun' is not to be connected with Amh. gämbär 'disk of the sun', as suggested by Praetorius 1879:510. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Bil. Qua. amäri 'morning', Kham. amir. Reinisch 1887:30 identifies it also with Sa.Af. abori, Or. aboro. For 'amir 'sun' from 'mr (see ammara, above) with a possibly basic meaning 'shine, be light', see Rundgren, Orientalia 32 (1963). 182. On a possible connection between 'amir and ayr 'sun', see Leslau 1956:186.
pl. of marh (see marha).
from Gk. amasenith, transcription of Heb. 'al haš-šäminït; also Syr. 'ansawnïwan (in Payne Smith 239).

meaning: (T.M.) 'the eastern door of a church, gateway of a church compound';
origin_explanation: for the terms connected with the church ending in -ähi "in parte mistïche", see Guidi 1906:921.
could it be an interpretation of the proper name Amos, from Heb. 'amos?

SEMITIC: Ar. 'amat 'bondswoman, slave girl', SAR. mt, Heb. 'ämä(h), Aram. 'am(t), Syr. 'am(t)a, Md. 'amta, Ph. mt, Ug. 'amt, Akk. amtu. On this biradical root, see Noeldeke 1910:129-30. Note that in Geez the final -t is considered a radical of the root if one judges by the plural 'amät. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'amät 'handmaid, slave girl', Amh. amät.

meaning: (pl. 'amätät) 'cubit, forearm, (T.Y.M) measure by cubits'; 'ammata (Y), 'ammata (T) 'measure by cubits' (denominative);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SAR. mt (pl. mm, mn) 'cubit', Soq. emeh, Heb. 'amma, anc. Aram. mh, Syr. 'ammätä, amt, Md. ama, Akk. ammatu. ETHIOPIC: Te. ammät 'forearm, cubit', Tna. mät, Amh. amät (from Geez) passed into Cush. Som. amä'amad. For the measure of the 'amat see Pankhurst 1969 1:32.
note that T has amotarge, name of a country.
from Gk. amethystos, acc. amethyston 'amethyst'.

SEMITIC: Ar. 'ana, SAR. n, Heb. 'ani, anc. Aram. 'ana, Syr. 'ena, Md. ana, Ph. n, Ug. an; augmented by k in Heb. 'anoki, Aram. nk, Ph. nk, Ug. ank, Akk. anaku. For more details, see Brockelmann 1908:297-9; Barth 1913:3. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'ana, Tna. 'ane, Amh. 'ane, Har. 'än, Gur. 'anä, Arg. ay; also in Cushitic: Bil.Qua.Kham, an, Be. ane, Sa.Af. anu.
for this element, see *na- 'anbala, and cp. Te. 'änka 'take!', Amh. 'änka.
from Heb. hin through Greek transcription ein; cp. also Ug. hn 'liquid measure'.

meaning: with suffixes 'anbäya 'I am not in a position, I am not able, no, I refuse'; with other suffixes 'anbäkku, 'anbäkku 'be unwilling, refuse, reject'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: 'anb- is a compound of the negative element 'an and the preposition b 'in'. For the negative 'an, cp. Ar. 'in, Heb. 'en, Samaritan-Aram. 'in, Syr. 'an, Ug. yanu 'there is not'. For 'an in Ethiopic and Ar. 'in, see Leslau, AION 29 (1969).137-145. ETHIOPIC: the negative 'an also occurs in 'anbäka 'don't know' (see below), in Amh. ank"an (see Praetorius 1879:259; Cohen 1939:324), and in the Gurage dialects. As for G. 'anbi, cp. Tna. 'anbi 'anbi bäla 'refuse', Amh. 'anbi 'alä; passed into Cushitic: Bil.Qua.Kham. embiy, Qem. enbiy. In the light of the analysis of G. 'anbi as 'an-b, it is doubtful that 'anbi is to be connected with 'abaya, as suggested by Leslau 1979:5 in connection with Gur. ebbä.
variant of h.anbäl, below.

meaning: preposition and conjunction: 'without, save, before, except for, except (that), excepting, with the exception of, (when) but, but also, rather, outside of, sides, apart from, regardless of, so that not' za'anbala, same; za'anbala 'ama 'except (that), unless (that)'; 'anbala dä'ämu 'but not, but'; za'anbala dä'ämu 'unless'; za'anbala za + verb 'but, except'; za'anbala kama + imperfect 'unless'; albo-za'anbala 'only'.
origin_explanation: the suffix pronouns are attached to the base 'anbale-; it is composed of 'an- (for which see also 'anbaya) and -bala. SEMITIC: Soq. di-bal 'without', Heb. bal 'not', Syr. balay 'without', Ug. bal 'not', Ph. bl, Akk. balu, bali 'without'. For bl augmented by t, cp. Heb. bilti 'without', Ph. blt 'except', SAR. bl-tn, bl-ty 'without'. As for Ar. bal, it expresses a denial after an interrogative or affirmative clause 'no, not at all, quite the contrary' (see Blachère 780; Lane 243). See also Cohen 1976:65. Dillmann's 1907:404 derivation of 'anbala from 'ambala is unlikely since mb does not become nb. Barth 1907:32 seems to connect G. 'anbala with Ar. balä; however, Ar. balä means 'yes, no doubt'. See also Rundgren 1955-23, 217, and passim; von Soden 380 connects 'an- of 'anbala with Akk. ina, 'in, in through'; cp. also Akk. ina bäli 'without'. Littmann 1964:372 considers, with reservations, 'an of 'anbala as a negative element. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'anbäl 'without', Tna. 'anbälä (for 'anbär, see Praetorius 1871:24), Amh. 'anbälä (from Geez). See also M. Cohen, GLECS 4 (1945-8).62-3.
from Amh. 'anbilta; also in Tna. 'anbilta.

meaning: (T.M) 'which is not extinguished, which does not disappear';
origin_explanation: the initial 'al seems to be the Arabic article, but the root is not Arabic.

SEMITIC: Dillmann 659 (following Ludolf) connects it with Ar. nabata 'gush forth'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'anbäta, 'anbäta 'locust'. Te. 'am-bäta, Amh.Arg.Gaf. 'anbäta, Gur. 'anbäta; also Cushitic: Aw. anbiti, Bil. anbata, Kham. abta. It may even be borrowed from Cushitic. Wajnberg 1937c:188 connects it with Amh. 'anbäṭ 'alä 'skip', but 'anbäṭ 'alä is undoubtedly a denominative. There is no connection between 'anbatä and Or. 'awänisa, as suggested by Praetorius, ZDMG 43 (1889).321-2. Brockelmann 1950:28 thinks that the Cushitic root is taken from Semitic.

meaning: (T) = Amh. 'anboṭä 'honey made from the blooms of the gaġġa-grass', (KG) = Amh. sälboṭ, sälboṭ 'kind of grass'.

origin_explanation: from Amh. 'anbuṭ.

meaning: anbayna, 'anbayna 'for the sake of, on account of, because of, on behalf of, about, concerning'; 'anbayna-za 'on account of this';
origin_explanation: from 'an- and bayna. For 'an, see 'anbala; for bayna, see below.
ETHIOPIC: From Cushitic: Sa. andādā 'skin', Be. adadā.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. andādī 'I don't know', Amh. angā (D'Abbadie 535 also andā); from the negative an (for which see anb-, above), and yd, yaydā 'know', below. The form andādī gives the impression of being a verbal noun with the suffix pronoun -ī of the 1st person, for which cp. anbi.

meaning: degenerate, illegitimate child, bastard, (T.M) dissolute, lustful, effeminate'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1103 derives it, with reservations, from a non-existent dabārā 'recede, be degenerate'. Dillmann 1907:27 connects it with Ar. dabara 'turn one's back' and states that "it appears to mean properly 'that which turns away from itself, that which abandons its nature'". ETHIOPIC: Amh. andābārā 'spoiled, unmannerly, rude' (undoubtedly from Geez). DTW 335 compares it with Amh. dabrahom 'minstrel, beggar'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. andahuļļā, andah"ahuļļā (for which see Strelcyn 1973:no. 79). Gur. andahuļļā.

From Lat. indictio through Gk. indiktion 'a period of fifteen years'; also Chr.-Ar. (ad-dawr) 'al-indīqṭī 'New Year', Syr. andeqṭīyōnā 'a space of fifteen years'. Amh. andoqṭayon 'the day in which light was created'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. andār, andur 'flute'; variant of anzi-rā.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. andārabbī, andārabbē 'malevolent or evil force'.
from Gk. enydridos, gen. sg. of enydris 'otter'
from Gk. antholops 'antelope'; see Hommel 1877:XXIX.

SEMITIC: Ar. anf 'nose, forepart of anything' f (pl. nf) 'façade' (W. Müller, ZAW 75[1962] Heb. 'ap 'nose', Aram. 'appē, Syr. 'appe 'face', Md. anpia 'face', Ug. ap, Akk. appu 'nose'. For other meanings of the root, see D. Cohen 1970:26. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'an(a)f 'nose', Tna. 'anfi, Gaf. Gur. āfuna, Har. ūf; 'blow the nose': Amh. annafā, Gur. āf barā; also in Cushitic: Kam. ye', Had. imfā-kko. Amharic also (tā)nāfännāfä 'angry, speak through the nose', Gur. (tā)nāf 'talk angrily', connected with nf 'nose' (cp. 1939:275); cp. also Heb. anep 'get angry'. Contrary to Cohen 1947:no. 35, Amh. afänqa 'nose' anfä(a) is from the root nff 'blow the nose' Leslau 1979:452) and is not to be connected with nf 'nose'.

meaning: anaphora, the part of the religious ceremony during which the Eucharistic elements are offered as an oblation, (T) priest (that is, the one who performs the anaphora)'
origin_explanation: from Gk. anaphorā 'Eucharistic offering'; also Chr.-Ar. nāfūr, nāfūra, Syr. anapōrā.

meaning: (mostly in questions) 'then indeed?, so then, so, surely, really, indeed, well now!' (Dillmann 1907:415);
origin_explanation: from an (see anka) and -gā; note that the meanings of angā come close to those of anka. Tna. anga 'in case', Gur. āga.
ang"a 'a break a bone to take out the marrow' (denominative); rastanag"a 'remove (the fat) from the marrow, suck the marrow'.

angädāwī 'that refers to a stranger or guest' a'angadā 'receive a guest, show hospitality, entertain' (denominative); ta'angadā 'travel about, become a stranger, emigrate, be a guest, lodge as guest'; yasta'angadā (Y.M) 'receive a guest, show hospitality'; ta'angado(t) 'hospitality, entertainment of guests, state of being a host, state of being a guest, emigration'; ETHIOPIC: from the root nagada 'travel' (see below). Also Tna. 'a-angädā 'receive a guest', angä-dā 'be a guest' (for which see Cohen 1939:253), Gaf. angädā 'guest', Har. nugda.
is a misprint for angäd"ā, below.

meaning: breast, chest, leather cuirass, breastplate, stole (for description, see motāhī)'
origin_explanation: malbasa angäd"ā 'breastplate' (see labsa).

meaning: wholesome, healthful, nag"a 'a be wholesome, be beneficial' with a basic meaning 'be pure, be clear', and for the passage of meaning he compares Ar. naqiya 'be pure' and niqiy 'marrow of a bone'. This derivation is doubtful.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Nyberg 232 derives it, with reservations, from Ar. nāğī, nāğī 'wholesome, healthful', nag"a 'a be wholesome, be beneficial' with a basic meaning 'be pure, be clear', and for the passage of meaning he compares Ar. naqiya 'be pure' and niqiy 'marrow of a bone'. This derivation is doubtful. ETHIOPIC: Te. angä'o 'marrow, best part of a thing'. Tna. ang"a, Amh. ang"a.

angeben (K*) 'offspring of an important family' ETHIOPIC: Amh. angebenayt 'offspring of an important family' (from Geez). On this enigmatic word, see Littmann 1913:39; J. Pirenne, JES 15 (1982) 113-5. The identification with Lat. ingenuus 'noble' suggested by Guidi, ZA 11 (1896). 413 is unlikely. A. Schall (in an unpublished paper) suggests either Ar. nağīb 'nobly-born', or Oromo hangafa, angafa 'first, first-born'.

meaning: for the development of meaning 'breast, breastplate', cp. Gk. thōrāks, Ar. hağafa 'breast, leather shield'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dietrich 168 suggests Ar. nağd 'breast'. Rabin 1975:90 suggests, with reservations, Heb. neğed 'in front'. Neither of these suggestions is satisfactory. ETHIOPIC: Tna. angäd"a 'back, backbone'. Dillmann 1203 quoting Ludolf connects the noun with G. g"ad"a 'beat, strike', that is, 'palpitation of the heart in the chest'.

meaning: (T) anāgunistis (Lt) anāgustis 'the minor order entrusted with the recitation of the lections and responsory psalms, ranking below the sub-deacons'
origin_explanation: anāg"anāstisāwī, same; also 'referring to the anāg"anāstis'. from Gk. anagnōstēs 'reader, member of minor order in church'; also Chr.-Ar. anāgnust, Syr. anagnōstā. ETHIOPIC: Tna. anag"astes, Amh. anag"anastis.
see also ang"at.

meaning: so, then!, therefore, wherefore, now then, forthwith, yet, and so' (see Dillmann 1907:414-5);
origin_explanation: anka-ssa 'then, therefore'; anka-ssa-ke 'then'; anka 'ādī 'any more'; ahatta anka 'once more, this once'; verb-ke anka 'now therefore'; wa-verb anka 'and so, therefore'; i-verb anka 'no longer, no more';
ETHIOPIC: From Cushitic: Sa. andādā 'skin', Be. adadā.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. andādī 'I don't know', Amh. angā (D'Abbadie 535 also andā); from the negative an (for which see anb-, above), and yd, yaydā 'know', below. The form andādī gives the impression of being a verbal noun with the suffix pronoun -ī of the 1st person, for which cp. anbi.

ETHIOPIC: Amh. andār, andur 'flute'; variant of anzi-rā.

from Gk. enydridos, gen. sg. of enydris 'otter'
from Gk. antholops 'antelope'; see Hommel 1877:XXIX.
