meaning: so, then!, therefore, wherefore, now then, forthwith, yet, and so' (see Dillmann 1907:414-5);
origin_explanation: anka-ssa 'then, therefore'; anka-ssa-ke 'then'; anka 'ādī 'any more'; ahatta anka 'once more, this once'; verb-ke anka 'now therefore'; wa-verb anka 'and so, therefore'; i-verb anka 'no longer, no more';
Dillmann 386 derives it from G. ansosawa 'walk slowly'; so also Praetorius 1879:157; M. Cohen, GLECS 5 (1948-51). 86.

phonetics: anast (pl. anəs) woman, wife, fe- male'; serves also as sex marker with animals: afras anast (or anəs) 'mares', adg anast 'she-ass'
meaning: woman, wife, female'; serves also as sex marker with animals: afras anast (or anəs) 'mares', adg anast 'she-ass'
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC. Ar. anta 'woman, female', SAr. n-t, T. Mh. tet (for tēt; Bittner 1916-21), Heb. issa (< *insā), Aram. intəṭā, ittəṭā, Syr. attəṭā, Md. anta, anat, Ph. st, Ug. at-t, Akk. assatu (< ansatu). ETHIOPIC. Tna. anastəyā, anastīyā 'woman', Te. assit (for ensit), anas 'female'. Amh. anast, anast 'wom- an', Gur. anast, asta, Har. anasti, Gaf. ansatā, Arg. ansəča. For its connection with ns, see nsəsa, below.
from Ar. an-nasī 'intercalary days of the Coptic year'.

phonetics: ansawā I, ansewā, ansowā (pl. anāsut) mouse, weasel'
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC: The connection with SAr. ndw 'destroy', Heb. nāsā (nsy) 'fall in ruins' (that is, 'be destroy- ed') suggested by Nyberg 233 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ansawa 'mouse', Te. ansay, ansay, Arg. henf; from Cushitic: Bil. Qem. ansawa, Qua. ensewā, Sa.Af. andawa, Kham. asawa (so also Prae- torius, ZDMG 47[1893].338). Amharic ayi 'mouse' can go back to *ansay > ayi (with n be- coming zero). In case it does not go back to *ansay, cp. Akk. ayasu 'weasel'. See also hansawā, hanse.
seems to be a misspelling of arot, or the other way around.

it also serves as a vocative element; e.g. ahuya 'you, my brother!' SEMİTİC. masculine: Ar. anta, SAr. nt, Md. heb. attā, Aram. antā, Syr. a(n)t, Md. an at, Akk. atta; feminine: Ar. anti, Soq. h att, Aram. nty, Syr. atti, Akk. atti. For details, see Barth 1913:8-10. ETHIOPIC. masculine: Te. anta, Tna. anta ( 'you' (the pronoun 'you' is expressed by n Amh. antā, Gaf. ant, antā, Gur. atā (also for which see Akk. in, ina 'in, through'; see also anbala, anza) and a demonstrative element -i(a). Te. at 'on, in, by, with, because of', atta 'there', Amh. ba-antā 'for the sake of' (from G. also in Tna. māntā, bāntā (for bā-antā), mantā, with metathesis in Te. mātan 'for the sake of', Tna. matan. See also Reinisch 1909:149.

phonetics: anta I 'through, by way of, by, at, into, to, in the direction of, because' (see Dillmann 1907:401-2)
meaning: through, by way of, by, at, into, to, in the direction of, because'

phonetics: anta II (relative pronoun, feminine) 'who, that, which'
origin_explanation: the suffix pronouns are attached to the base anti-a; e.g. antiaya 'that (she) of mine' for the usage of the relative pronoun, see the mascu- line pronoun za. The final -t is a feminine marker but the initial an is difficult to explain. It is per- to be identified with Te. at anta 'while she is', intē 'she is' (so also Rundgren 1955:211). In 1913:109 states, "Ich vermute einen berberi schen Stamm". There is the possibility of anta being common with Berber, but not taken from Berber.
for the masculine, see zakku it is composed of anta and -(k)ku, for which see below.
for the masculine, see zakku from anta augmented by -k, -t; see also -(k)ku.

meaning: be impatient, resent, be irritated, be indignant, be weary of, feel annoyed, feel disgust, (K*) be idle, loaf'; tavantalo 'impatience, spite, disgust, annoy- ance, (K*) idleness'; (amtaväntalo 'accidentally'); antāl (K*) 'imatience'; antāle (Gr) 'repugnance, disgust, indignation'; matavantal, (T) matavantal 'impatient, (T) who grumbles'
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC. Ar. atala 'walk in a disordered way (man who has lost his temper), get angry' (Rūžička 155).
ETHIOPIC. Te. Tna. antālam 'a dry measure'. For more details on antalam, see Pankhurst 1969. 11:117.

SEMİTİC. Ar. antumu, Soq. tin, Heb. attem (from antem), Aram. antun, Syr. attun, Md. anatun, Ug. atm, Akk. attu. For more details, see Barth 1913:10ff. ETHIOPIC. Te. antum, Tna. antun, attum 'o you!' ('you' is expressed by nassakaikum), Gur. atum; Amh.Gaf.Arg. annantā is formed with the mor- pheme of the plural anna + antā 'thou'. See also Leslau 1956:54. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Bil. entin, Qua. entan, Aw. antā, Sa. atin. For the singular, see anta.

SEMİTİC. Ar. antunna, Soq. ten, Heb. atten, Aram. Syr. attin, vatten, Akk. attina. For more details, see Barth 1913:10ff. ETHIOPIC. Te. antan, Tna. antan, attan 'o you!' ('you' is expressed by nassakaikən); Amh.Arg. Har.Gaf. Oriental Gur. same as masculine; for West Gur. and Soddo, see Leslau 1956:54.

meaning: the religious sect of the Antidicomarianites'
origin_explanation: from Gk. antidikomarianites; see antimaaryātos.
ETHIOPIC: Amb. anāṭoli 'young female camel'.
SEMITIC: probably to be connected with Ar. naṭla 'a small bit'. Or is there any connection with Tna. ʿanṭilug 'anṭilliq 'small portion'?

meaning: (K.Lt) 'hell, the inferno, the underworld, Tatar (legendary animal)'
origin_explanation: possibly from Gk. en tartarō 'in hell' (Salinger); see Amb. anṭorṭos (from Geez). See also barbaros.

meaning: 'while, whereas, when, even as, although, even though, since'; also used periphrastically as participle or adjective; e.g. rəʾəya kālʿān ənza yaqawwamu 'he saw others standing' (see Dillmann 1907:419, 484-5);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. ʿiḏā 'when', Sar. ʿiḏ. Dillmann 1907:419 analyzes G. ənza as ʿən-za; von Soden 380 compares ʿən of ʿənza with Akk. ʿina 'in, through'. Rundgren 1955:202 likewise gives to ʿən of ʿənza the meaning 'in'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ʿəndo 'while' (with a variant d:z that occurs in Tigre). Praetorius 1879:80 derives Amb. ayyä 'while' from ʿənza; so also Rundgren 1955:61.
since the Amharic equivalent is ḥəc anār one wonders whether anzar is not a misprint for anar.
from Amh. anzarṭ.

(T) 'friend'; M 404 translates it 'kind of demon' on the authority of Tayyä, but Tayyä renders it with Amh. wädäg 'friend'. Note that friend could be a euphemistic expression for 'demon'; cp. also ʿarākṭ 'friend' (fem) used euphemistically for 'snake'. See also ʿēp II;
perhaps corrupt from Gk. presbyteros 'elder, priest'; see also ʿarpāpe.
from Gk. epideksion, gen. of epideksios 'auspicious';

meaning: name of the eleventh Coptic month (corresponds to G. ḥamle)
origin_explanation: from Coptic epēp. See also ʿabib.
from Gk. epiphaneia 'Epiphany'; also Amh. ǝppifānayā.
note that Gk. apologos means 'fable, story'.
from Gk. apolopou, gen.sg. of apoloipos 'the remaining over'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. appoliṭu 'sparks of burning soot on the butt of a griddle'. See also ʿabolisu.
from Gk. apollyōn 'a destroying one'; also Amh. appolayon 'destructive enemy'.
from Gk. apēliōtēn, acc. sg. of apēliōtēs, same.
it is a combination of two words: ǝppomiḍā poḍerē; for ǝppomiḍā (from Gk. epōmis, acc. sg. epōmida), see ǝppemos, below; for poḍerē, see below.
(M.407 wrongly renders Tayyä's abnā bārād [the Amharic translation of ǝppemiḍār] 'hail'; in fact it also means 'marble').
from Gk. epōmides (nom. pl. of epōmis) 'shoulder'; it renders Heb. kāṭēpōṭ 'shoulders, sides'. OT 585 misunderstood Ez. 41:2 and wrongly rendered ǝppemedā by 'width of door'.

meaning: (Li) ǝppomido 'priestly garment made of two pieces joined on the shoulder' (translation of the Heb. ǝpōd).
origin_explanation: from Gk. epōmis (acc. sg. epōmida), same.
from Gk. epopa (acc. of epops) 'hoopoe'. See also heppopā.
from Gk. aparkhē 'firstling'; also Chr.-Ar. ʿabarkā 'wine used for the Mass'. See also ʿappargayos, and ʿaṭārgōn.
probably rather 'animal sacrifice', and see ʿupporge.

liqa ʿǝppis qoppos 'archbishop'; ʿǝppis qopposǝnnā 'function of the bishop, episcopacy'; From Gk. episkopos 'overseer, bishop'; also Te. ʿǝppisqoppos, Amh. ǝppisqoppos. See also kore ʿǝppisqoppos, ʿabusqubus, ʿēbis kobos.
from Gk. abaton 'inaccessible room of a temple'.
from Gk. epaoidōn (gen. pl. of epaoidos) 'enchanter'.
from Gk. klēmatidōn (gen. pl. of klēmatis) 'vine-branch'.
from Gk. ekklēsia 'assembly, church'; also Syr. ʿeqlīšyā, Amh. aqlǝsāyā.
from Gk. hēgemonos, gen. sg. of hēgemōn 'leader, bishop'. See also qomos.

meaning: (T.M) ʿaqān, (D 1397) ʿaqān 'thistle, thorn, (T) low-growing plant, parasitic plant'
origin_explanation: from Gk. akan 'thistle'.

meaning: (Lt) 'white linen'; perhaps from Gk. argēs 'shining, white'.
origin_explanation: perhaps from Gk. argēs 'shining, white'.

meaning: (T), name of a star; perhaps from Syr. 'aryā 'Leo, the sign of the zodiac for August'.
origin_explanation: perhaps from Syr. 'aryā 'Leo, the sign of the zodiac for August'.

SEMITIC: Ar. raḥa 'palm of hand', Soq. riḥoḥ, Heb. raḥat 'winnowing shovel', Ug. rḥt-m 'hands' (dual), Akk. rittu 'hand' (Holma 119). Brockelmann 1908: 230 compares Syr. laḥeṭā 'palm of hand' (with alternance of liquids). ETHIOPIC: Tna. raḥat 'palm of hand' is borrowed from Arabic. The comparison with Sa. ḥarā 'hand' suggested by Reinisch 1890:190 is unlikely.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. arkärseṃton (from Geez). See ak-rasmeṇton.

meaning: (T) 'who closes the gate'; corrupt from Gk. pylōros 'doorkeeper'. See also ʾiflusāwiyān.
origin_explanation: corrupt from Gk. pylōros 'doorkeeper'. See also ʾiflusāwiyān.

meaning: (T.Y.M) 'weed, correct'; from Amh. arrāmā; also in Te. ʾarrāma 'weed out, correct', Tna. ʾarrāmā 'passed into Cushitic: Bil. arām 'weed', Kham. arem.
origin_explanation: from Amh. arrāmā; also in Te. ʾarrāma 'weed out, correct', Tna. ʾarrāmā 'passed into Cushitic: Bil. arām 'weed', Kham. arem.

meaning: 'heathen, pagan, non-Christian, infidel, Greek, gentile, (Lt) Muslim'.
origin_explanation: arāmāwī, aramay, same; also 'speaker of Aramaic'.
from Heb. ʾarammī (Ullendorff 1968:122), while armayā has a Syriac (ʾarmāyā) form; also in Te. Tna. ʾarāmawi. See Noeldeke 1910:35, n. 4, and ʾaramane.

meaning: (T) 'note that ʾarmodada rendered by D 1404 ṣaruf 'offended, abused' is wrong since ʾarmodada is a verb in the perfect, perhaps originally that makes things quiet or bal- (see rmm II, ʾarmama).
origin_explanation: note that ʾarmodada rendered by D 1404 ṣaruf 'offended, abused' is wrong since ʾarmodada is a verb in the perfect, perhaps originally that makes things quiet or bal- (see rmm II, ʾarmama).
ETHIOPIC: Amh. wärdāmāne 'uneducated' (from Geez). See also ʾarami.
see ʾarmāsaqos.

meaning: (L) ʾormisqos ʾarmisqos (Lt) ʾarnesqos, ʾarnesqos 'small necklace, chain, bead'.
origin_explanation: from Gk. hormiskos 'small necklace, chain, bead'. See also ʾarmes.
perhaps to be identified with ʾerar 'sky', below.
SYR: Syr. ʾarnā 'mountain goat' considered a loanword from Akkadian (so Jensen in Brockelmann 1928:50); perhaps Soq. ʾerehon 'goats', Sh. ʾerun.
SEMITIC: Ar. ʾarnab 'hare', Heb. ʾarnāḇ, Aram. Md. arnab, Ug. ʾnhb, Akk. annabu, annabu. ETHIOPIC: Gur. arbānnā 'hare', Har. harbaño. The Semitic-Ethiopic root is perhaps an Arabic loanword.
see ʾapārupe.

meaning: (T.D 1404,OT) 'thorn, acacia, forked pole, wooden instrument used in preparing thread for weaving'.
origin_explanation: corrupt from Gk. barkēnīm, transcription of Heb. ʾargēṇīm 'thorns'. See also barāgenīm.
from one of the various cases of Gk. ak- 'made of juniper'.

ʾarara, causative; taʾarara, taʾarara, passive; tǝʾrir 'harvest, crop'; māʾarar 'harvest, what is harvested (produce, fruit), vintage, summer, autumn'; a secondary form of ʾaraya, below. ETHIOPIC: Amh. arrārā 'reap, mow', marrārā 'gather the harvest, glean' (denominative from rār); also Cushitic: Som. urur 'crowd, mass', Sa.Af. arar 'glean'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. erār 'sky' (from Geez), see ʾirnā, above.

meaning: 'field, (K*) a big tree, wild date palm, (KG) pomegranate'.
origin_explanation: from Gk. arouran, acc. sg. of aroura 'field'.

meaning: 'mode of chant dedicated to the Holy Ghost'; it is used on ordinary days and also for the main holidays of the year.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. araray, Te. ʾarārā. Amh. araray passed into Cushitic: Bil. erērā. The noun is onomatopoetic if it is connected with Amh. arrāy 'cry for help', arr 'cry for chasing away goats'. For the other modes, see E. Welles, "Studien zur äthiopischen Kirchenmusik", OC 9 (1920).74-106, Tito Lepisa 163, Velat 76, 78, 686.

meaning: (K), name of the moon; see also ʾerā.
'device for bringing water from a well'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. arasīb, same.
from Gk. arkhimandritēs 'principal of a group of monks'; also Chr.-Ar. ʾarsī matridās.
see also ʾarsos.
taʾarsasa (Gr.462) 'become a heretic, be a heretic'; from Gk. hairesis 'heresy' through Chr.-Ar. ʾarāsis; also Syr. ʾeresis, ḥeresis. See also ʾalsis.
from Gk. arsis 'lifting, what is lifted, load'. See also ʾarson.
could it be a wrong form for ʾarṣaṭābu, ʾarasṭāb?
from Gk. astrolabos 'armillary sphere'; also Amh. arasṭalab. See also ʾasqolobā, ʾasṭālab.
from Gk. Aristotelēs, Aristotle, considered a wizard.
see ʾarsimatradās.
from Gk. arkhiereus 'high priest'; also Chr.-Ar. ʾarsī ʾarūs.
Source: Amh. arrātā 'tie a string, package by tying'

meaning: = Amh. doqma 'tree of the temperate highlands'
'the Law, the Ten Commandments, the Octateuch (that includes the Pentateuch and Joshua, Judges and Ruth)'; 'ortāwi 'referring to orit, devoted to Mosaic laws'
origin_explanation: from Aram.-Syr. ōraytā.
from Gk. orthodoksos 'orthodox' through Chr.-Ar. 'urtaduks; also Syr. ortodoksā, Tna. 'artodoksawi.

meaning: = Amh. wanza 'Cordia Abyssinica' (for which see Strelcyn 1973: no. 263).

phonetics: artābos አርታቦስ, artibās አርቲባስ አርቲባስ አርቲባስ (T)

origin_explanation: From Ar. 'artāl, pl. of ratl (of Gk. [litra] origin). See also latr.
metathesis of 'wr, ta'awara, below.

meaning: 'cloak of dark blue silk, blue silk'
origin_explanation: from Amh. arwa, any kind of silk stuff.

meaning: 'animal, wild animal, beast, wild beast, reptile'; 'arwe madr 'snake, serpent, dragon'; 'arwe haris 'unicorn, rhinoceros'; 'arwe zabart 'silver dish in the form of a snake'

origin_explanation: SAr. 'rwy-n, 'arye 'lion' (for which see Wagner 29-30), Syr. 'aryā, Md. aria, Akk. arwû 'buck (of or mountain goat)'. Koehler 86 considers it an 'African' loanword without giving, however, the name of the African language. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'arawit, 'arā 'wild animal' 'serpent', Amh.Arg.Gaf.Gur. awre 'wild animal' (with metathesis), Gur. also awre, name of a spirit that possesses a person, Har. ūri 'wild animal'; also in Cushitic: Som. ār 'lion'. See 'awrāhars, 'ahur.

meaning: = Amh. sansa, either 'pole which two men carry on their shoulders in order to transport a heavy object' or 'food plant'
see ta'arwaya 'become brutal, become wild, become savage, become angry, rise against like a wild animal'
asta'arwaya, 'asta'arawa 'render wild'; 'arwuy (fem. 'arwit) 'wild, fierce, savage'; ta'arwayo(t) 'cruelty, fierceness, savageness'
origin_explanation: the verb is a denominative from 'arwe, above.

meaning: pass. part. of 'araya; also (KG) = Amh. kardād 'kind of grass'

origin_explanation: Ph. 'ry, 'ūry 'granary, storehouse'. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'arā (ry) 'gather, glean, yā; also in Cushitic: Aw.Bil. ar 'gather, glean'. See also 'arara.

meaning: 'consult a sorcerer to find out the future, have a prediction made'

origin_explanation: referring to the gateway of the church; T renders it wrongly by 'gateway of the church'; from Gk. hōraiā, fem.sg. of hōraios 'young, fresh, beautiful'.
from Gk. ōriōn through Ar. *'ūriyūn confusing the final ن and ب; also in Amh. aryob.

meaning: 'heights, the high, the highest, highest heaven, seventh heaven, (Gr.158) kind of zemā-chant'; 'aryāmāwi 'sublime, celestial, lofty'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Heb. rāmīm (pl. of rām) 'the high heaven' (Dahood 1968:247). ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'aryam 'highest heaven, zemā-chant', Amh. aryam 'seventh heaven, rym, below.
from Gk. ōriōn; see also 'aryob.

explained by Dillmann 774 as coming from Hebr. 'ōr 'light of the sun' (so also J. Halévy, JA 9[1867],385); perhaps rather a variant of 'orāyānos and to be derived from Gk. ouranos 'sky > sun'. See also 'irnā, 'aryos.

SEMETIC: Ar. 'arz, Heb. 'erez, Aram.-Syr. 'arzā, Ug. arz. For a Hittite origine of 'rz, see 1964:5. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'arz, Amh. Arz. Noeldeke states that G. 'arz (also Ar. 'arz) is borrowed from Syr. 'arzā.
perhaps from Gk. hiereus 'priest' (Müller). See 'arzo.
from Ar. 'ās borrowed from Aram.-Syr. 'āsā according to Fraenkel 139. In other Semitic languages: Heb. 'āsā (*), Md. asa, Akk. asu.
from Ar. 'al-'asad 'lion'.

phonetics: asfālito, asfalitā, asfalit, asfalitos አስፋሊቶ, አስፋሊታ, አስፋሊት, አስፋሊቶስ (T) asfalitā, asfalito አስፋሊቶ
origin_explanation: from Gk. asphaltos 'pitch'; also Syr. 'aspalton, Amh. asfālit, asfalit. See also 'aflātis.

phonetics: asfalutu, asfalitu, asfalit, asfalito አስፋሉቱ, አስፋሊቱ, አስፋሊት, አስፋሊቶ (T.Sw.in D 753)
origin_explanation: perhaps from Gk. asphalton 'clover' (Müller).
from Heb. 'āsūppīm through Gk. esephim.

meaning: 'how much? how many? how long?, how often?'
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1907:361 derives 'asfantu from an interrogative > and safantu 'what is the size of?', in connection with safan, properly 'prominence, size'. Praetorius 1890:370-1 derives 'asfantu from 'afō-sant 'who 'how?' in Geez; sant 'how much?' in Amharic, from wsn > *'afsant > 'asfant, by metathesis). Rundgren 1955:40ff analyzes 'asfantu as coming from *'af-san -tu, san representing s lengthened by n, the underlying form being 'ay-fā-sa-n-tu. It is more likely to be derived from safn 'size' with a prothetic ' and a suffix -tu by analogy with the numerals ending in -tu. See also Gur. mast (Leslau 1979:431), and -tu.
from Ar. asfar 'yellow'; it could then refer to jaundice (cp. also Ar. asfar 'jaundice'); also Te. asfar 'yellow', Amh. asfar 'fever that makes one haggard'. See also säfra, zafrā, asfar, asfārs.
from Ar. 'asfar 'yellow' (see also asfar, above). As for 'precious stone', it could come from Gk. sapphei- ros 'lapis lazuli'.

meaning: baker's loaf, date-cake, (OT) piece of meat';
origin_explanation: transcription of Heb. 'espār 'date-cake'.

phonetics: asfarēdā, asfarēdā, asfarīdā, asfarīdā, asfa- rid, asfarēd
origin_explanation: from Gk. spyridda, acc. sg. of spyris 'basket'; also in Syr. 'esparīdā.
see asfar.
'abna ashatayā 'crystal stone'. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. 'ashayta 'hoarfrost', Amh. asha- tya (from Geez).

meaning: until, till, unto, as far as, so much so, up to, to the point of, even to, even also, within, for'; also followed by other prepositions: 'aska la 'until, to', 'aska haba 'up to', 'aska wasta 'even into', 'aska 'ama 'until'; conjunction: 'so that, until, until that, to the point that, insomuch as, to the extent that, with the result that'; with prepositions: 'aska 'ama 'till when, till the time that, so long as', 'aska soba 'till that, insomuch (that), so that'. 'aska-na, 'aska-ne, same as 'aska; with suffix pronouns, as in 'askanehu 'until there, until it'.
origin_explanation: from 'on-sa-ka > 'assaka > 'aska, need to introduce the element 'on 'an elsewhere as in 'anza; see also R 28-32. Dillmann's 1907:395 identifies with 'ad-ka is unlikely. Likewise identification of 'as (of 'aska) with Ar. suggested by Praetorius 1879:269. 1912:77 connects 'aska with Bil. -sik, suffixed to a verb to express a con- Brockelmann 1950:38 considers 'aska from the Cushitic suffixes -sik, -zik. A (1937), 71 identifies it with Ar. 'idāk > 'aska. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'asak 'until', Amh. as iant astā, Gaf. astā--salā 'until'. Run connects Tna. kasa 'up to, until' w.

meaning: (used with the jussive or impera- tive) 'here is, lo and behold!, final- then, I beg you!, o now!, o that please!';
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1907:120 compares -ku of of 'akko (see however 'akko, above) and as 'as-ku for sa-ku with a prothetic 1955:33 considers the s of 'as to be a de.

meaning: eskedre (D 1405), 'eskedre (T) ' top of column, (OT) house of priest for offerings';
origin_explanation: from Gk. exsedra 'hall fitted with se eskedar, eskare.
see asquf.

meaning: grape(s), cluster (of grapes, dates), bunch of fruit';
origin_explanation: 'askāla wayn 'a grape'; (māya 'askāl 'wine'); 'askālōn (T) 'cluster of grapes' (see SEMITIC: Ar. 'itkal, 'utkāl, 'itkāl 'boug clusters', Heb. 'eskōl 'cluster of berries', lā, Ug. 'skl (Virolleaud, GLECS 7[1954] is/šunnatu 'bunch of grapes' (von Soder that Baumgartner 92 quoting J.J. Stamm, the Akka- dicus Namengebung (1930), p. 255, n. 6. considers,
is either a variant of 'askaroni (see below consists of 'askāl, above, and the enclitic -ni.
lahsa 'askemā 'monastic dress'; from Gk. skhēma 'dress'; also Chr.-Ar. 'iskim, also Tna. 'askema, Amh. askema.
'eskapape (P) 'field'; from Gk. skapane 'digging tool, spade' (Müller). The form of the verb is strange. See also hesqapa.
see 'eskedera.

meaning: askarem 'chest, coffer, casket, bag, store, treasury, (Lt) coffin, corpse, skeleton';
origin_explanation: from Lat. scrinium through Gk. skrinion ' chest'; also Tna. 'askāren 'corpse, skeleton', askāren.
perhaps to be identified with 'askālōn (see above) with alternance of liquids.

meaning: pubic hair, a creeper from the seeds of which children make bracelets';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. asākt, asākt, same. In the sense 'pubic hair', it is perhaps a variant of 'askit (see below).

meaning: testicle, geni- talia (of man or woman)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'asak 'labia of the vulva' (D. Cohen 1970:35, with reservations), Heb. 'ešek 'testicle', Syr. 'eškā, Ug. 'ušk, Akk. 'išku. ETHIOPIC: Amh. askit 'testicle' (from Geez). See also asakt.
'aslama 'profess Islam, become a Muslim'; 'aslāmāy (Gr.161) 'Muslim'; from Ar. 'islām. See also maslam.

meaning: because, since, in that, that is, namely, indeed, in fact'; it also serves to intro- duce a quotation;
origin_explanation: there are some who connect it with sam with a prothetic s while others see in sm a deictic element. Thus Praetorius 1890:378 derives it from sam 'name' with a prothetic 's and compares Egyptian- Arabic ('-)ism 'an 'unter dem Namen dass > weil' (so also Bittner, WZKM 15[1901],115). Barth 1893:59 derives it from 'n (cp. Akk. ina 'when') and sam. Likewise Brockelmann 1908:496 (follow- ing Jensen) analyzes 'asma as coming from 'on- sama > 'assama > 'asma. The analysis of 'asma as being composed of deictic elements s-m is suggested by Eitan, AJSL 44 (1927-8),185 who analyzes it as *sa-ma 'that [is] what' or 'why' strengthened by a prothetic 's. Dillmann 1907:119 analyzes 'asma as coming from 's (which he calls 'prefixed aspirate') and sa-ma 'that which'. Rundgren 1955:24 derives 'asma from 'sn (for 'sn, see 'onsa-s) (enclitic ele- ment) -ma; or from sa with a prothetic 's (that is, 'asa) and -ma. I personally am inclined to connect 'asma with sam 'name', cp. also Akk. aššum < ana šum 'concerning, because of', Aram. bšm 'because of' (for which see Kutscher, JAOS 74[1954],242). W. Müller adds German 'Name, nämlich'.

from Heb. 'isōn yāh where 'isōn is a diminutive of 'is and yāh merely an intensive termination. This is the name of the moon in connection with its likeness to the human face" (so Halévy, quoted in R.H. Charles, Henoch 208). See also 'asbanyā.

meaning: (T.M) 'stone marten' (animal about the size of a cat which makes burrows); see also ›asbāt.
(T) = Amh. asē 'tree of the hot lowlands which has edible berries'.
(T.M) 'ball of wood or woven leather strips used in hockey game'.
transcription of Gk. athanīm, from Heb. yerah hā-›ētānīm explained as the month in which formerly the overflowing rivers still have water left. See also ›atānīm, ›ethanīm.
origin_explanation: transcription of Gk. athanīm, from Heb. yerah hā-›ētānīm
from Ar. ›attūn < Aram.-Syr. ›attūnā, Md. ›attuna < Akk. ›atūnu (Zimmern 32). ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. ›aton, Amh. ›aton.
from Coptic (Bohairic) athōr. See also hātur.
perhaps to be identified with Aram. turgamānā 'translation' (see targ™ama), or with ›atrikon.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. atrikon (from Geez). See also ›atrikon.
from Gk. anthrakos, gen. sg. of anthraks 'coal, precious stone of dark color'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. ›atranos 'reading desk'. See also ›atrons, ›atrons.
from Gk. khronos 'seat'. See also ›atron, ›atrons.

meaning: 'be removed, be taken away, withdraw, (Lt) faint'; ›atata 'remove, take away, put away, put aside, turn away, put off, cast away, dismiss, with draw, dispel, displace'; ta›atata, ta›attata, pass. of ›atata; also 'disap pear, absent oneself, depart from, recede'; ›asta›atata (Lt) 'remove'; ›atāti (K*), act. part. of ›atata; ›atātīt (fem. ›atātīt) 'removed, taken away, dis tant'; ›atuta 'separately, apart'; ›atātē 'removal'; ›a›tāti, part. of ›atata; ta›atātī (Gr.463) 'displaced, removed'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the comparison with Soq. ›oti 'weak' sug gested by Leslau 1938:77 is unlikely since G. ›atata 'faint' (as given ib.) is a secondary meaning. ETHIOPIC: Amh. attātā 'remove, take away'.

meaning: 'come, come home, go home, return home, return'; ›atawa 'bring, bring home, bring in, bring about, yield, send home, let go home'; (›atawa ›amanna 'bring away'); ›atwat, ›atot (n) 'coming home, return home, return, yield (of crops)'; mə›tāw 'place to which one returns home, domicile, return, going (n)';
origin_explanation: from Ar. ›ata 'come, arrive', Sar. ›rw, Soq. ›tw.
Heb. ›ātā (tw/y). Aram. ›ātā, Syr. ›etā, Md. ›ata, Ug. ›tw. ETHIOPIC: Te. ›ata (›tw) 'enter, come, re turn'. Tna. ›atāwā; also in Cushitic: Bil.Qua. Dem. ent, Kham.
transcription of Heb. ›attiwāqēhā (Ez. 41:15).

meaning: (KG) = Amh. zāgba, a large tree resem bling the cedar.

origin_explanation: to be identified with ›itayā, below, from Gk. itea 'willow'.
corrupt from tarbentos, below.
from Ar. ›atlas 'satin'; also Amh. atlas 'black velvet or satin', Har. atlās 'silk'.
related to asq II. As for ›atqu, it is not be considered a foreign word as suggested by Dillmann 805 following Ludolf.
M 395 misunderstood Amh. q™antərra 'wingless locust' (the translation of G. ›atāqān given by Tayyä) as being derived from q™anātara

meaning: 'take a small amount of bread, pinch'; and therefore, wrongly renders G. ›agāqān by 'fist, small amount, pinch';
origin_explanation: from Gk. attakēs, acc. sg. of attakēs 'locust'. See also ›agāqān.
scribal error for ›apārgon (D 1406); from Gk. aparkhē, gen. pl. of aparkhē 'firstling'. See also ›apargē.
misprint for ›anfaya (see nfy).
SEMITIC: Ar. ›aw 'or', Sar. ›w, Heb.Aram. ›aw, ›ō, Syr. ›aw, Md. ›u, Ug. ›u, Akk. ū. ETHIOPIC: Te. ›aw 'or', Har. aw; related to Tna.Amh.Gaf. wāy, Gur. we; also Cushitic: Sa. ō.

meaning: 'yes, of course, certainly, indeed, even so'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (dialectal) ›awwa, ›ēwa 'yes', Mh. aywa, ewe. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ›awā 'yes', Te. ›ewā, Amh. awo, awon, Gur. āwo. While phonetically it is possible to derive Amh. awo, awon 'yes' from the root mn 'believe' (G. ›amān 'true', with weakening of m, as suggested by Praetorius 1879:27), there is no need for this derivation since awo, aw has the nature of
from Gk. aukhmōdēs 'dry, arid'.
corrupt from Gk. synaksaria, nom., acc. pl. of synaksarion.
from Gk. Ōkeanos 'ocean', also Syr. uqiyānos, Aram. uqiyāyānos. See also wəqəyānos.

SEMITIC: Ar. wal ('wl) 'vapor that rises mornings and evenings'. ETHIOPIC: Te. wəla ('wl) 'be moist', 'awlə 'rainy season (in the lowland)', Tna. 'awəl 'humid', 'awlə 'dew'. Amh. perhaps also awlə n əfās 'storm' (Kape-liuk).

meaning: 'olive tree, oak, wood from olive tree'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'awlə 'wild olive tree'; from Cushitic: Sa. ōlə.
could be a metathesis of 'awlə, below.

meaning: 'the blessed bread which is distributed among the clergy at the end of the Mass'
origin_explanation: from Gk. eulogia 'blessed bread', through Chr.-Ar. 'awləgiyā; also Tna. 'awləgiyā 'eulogy', Amh. awlə-giyā.
from Gk. aulōn 'hollow between hills'; also Syr. 'awlānā 'valley, open land'. See also 'əlonā.
see 'əlyətōn.
could it be a transcription of Heb. 'āwən 'wicked-ness, uncanny words'?
see also 'əwəra (above), unless the root is tə'əwəra and is to be identified with 'wr 'be blind'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. haris, 'əwrə'əris, Amh.Gur. 'əwrə'əris. Ar. is considered a Geez loanword (Noeldeke 1910:57). See also 'ərwe.
see also əwrəqis.
from Gk. eurakylōn 'north-east wind'; also Syr. 'əwəgəlīdōn. See also 'əwrəngi.
transcribes Gk. horāsis 'appear-ance'; the lexicographers misunderstood it and translated it 'stars (T.M), circling of the stars (OT), splendor' (K).
denominative of 'əwre, metathesis of 'əwre, above.
from Gk. aurion 'tomorrow'.
tə'əwəsa (T.M) 'be spilled, be diluted'; related to hws, hosa 'move, shake'.
see also 'əwəst.
the derivation of əwəst from 'əwəst and its being borrowed from Heb. 'ayət 'bird of prey', 'lion', suggested by Dillmann 1907:306, is unlikely. See also 'əwsənt.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. aw alā 'cry, bark (hyena)'. Amh. awwāwā 'howl'; related to 'əwyawa, below.
yohannəs 'əwīt 'hymn for St. John the Baptist'.
perhaps a misunderstanding for 'əwtār 'cords of a tent'.
it translates Amh. təqur 'əwī 'plante de la Solanacées' (Strelcyn 1968:86; 1973:no. 37).
from Ar. hayt (Bezold, Kebra Negest, p. 92, n. 5).
from 'əwte badl (badal), for which see below.
refers to Eutyches, the heretic, ca. 378-454 (see F.L. Cross, The Oxford Dictionary of the Christian Church, 1974, p. 484).
see 'əwyawa, and 'əwet, above.

meaning: 'which?, what?, what kind?, what sort of?, what a!'; indefinite 'whoever, whatever'
origin_explanation: with prepositions: bə'əy 'in which?, in what?, by what?, at what?'; lə'əy 'for which?', 'əm'əy 'from which?', for which?'; with suffix pronouns: 'əyātəmu 'which of them?'; see Dillmann 1907:119, 335-6; SEMITIC: Ar. 'əyy 'which?', SAR. 'y 'whatsoever'. Mh. ey, Heb. 'ē 'where?', 'ē zē 'which?', Aram. 'ē 'what?, where?, how?', 'ēkā 'where now?' (aug-mented by kā), Syr. 'əynā 'what?', 'əykā 'where?'. Ug. 'y 'any', Akk. ayyu 'which?'. See also Jensen 1889:422; Brockelmann 1908-327; Barth 1913: 143ff; D. Cohen 1976:16, under 'yy; Pennacchietti 98ff. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'əyi 'which?', Tna. 'əyyān, 'əyyənay, also in nəbāy 'whither?' (from nəb 'ay), kəmāy 'how!' (from kəmā 'ay), Har. 'əy 'which?', Gur. e 'where?', also in Cushitic: Aw.Sa.Af. 'ay 'who?, which?', Bil.Kham. aū, Qem. aw, Som. ē (see also Dolgopolsky 131).
'əyyobelyos, 'əyyobelyon, 'əyyobelyu 'jubilee year'; from Heb. yōbēl through Gk. iōbēl; also Syr. yūbə-lāyē 'jubilee year', Tna. 'əyobel, 'əyyubel, Amh. 'əyobe-lyu.

'əyhə 'drown with a flood' (denominative); 'əyhə (Y.T.M) 'destroy, drown' (tr.); tə'əyhə 'drown (intr.), (K) be covered with water'; ETHIOPIC: Te. mayā 'əyhə 'flood' (from Geez), Tna. 'əyhi, Amh. mayā 'əyoh (from Geez). W. Müller.

meaning: 'shovel, (T) spoon, (OT) a tool to rake a fire'
origin_explanation: From Gk. iamin, transcription of Heb. hay-yā'im 'shovels'. See also yāmīn.
SEMITIC: Ar. 'āna (wn) 'enjoy peacefulness, relax' (Dillmann 797).
see 'appargayon.
From Gk. eikona, acc. sg. of eikōn 'picture, icon, likeness' through Chr.-Ar. 'iqūna, 'ayqūna, qūna; also Aram. 'iqōnā, Syr. yuqnā.
'iyyar 'air, sky, the highest sky'; 'ayyarāwī 'aerial, of the air, ethereal'; from Gk. aēr 'air'; also PbH. 'āwīr, Aram. 'āwīrā, Syr. 'āwār, Md. aiār. ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. 'ayyār, Amh. 'ayyār.

meaning: 'ayyār, name of the second Hebrew month'
origin_explanation: from Heb. 'iyyār, through Chr.-Ar. 'ayyār, Akk. ayaru.
probably for 'ayyur 'reviled, insulted, offended' (see 'ayyur II). ETHIOPIC: Amh. ayyur, same. It is difficult to know whether Amh. ayyur is borrowed from Geez or the other way around.

meaning: 'ayasa (Sw. in D 796), 'esa (Lt) 'waste away, languish, (T.Y.D.796) be turbulent, rave, be confounded, boil boil over, quarrel, go here and there looking for something, groan in distress, (Lt) soften, grow soft'; ta'esa (D 1397) 'be patient'; 'is 'roaming here and there' (n.)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'ayisa 'despair of'; related to Heb. yə'iš (nif'al), Ar. ya'isa.
one recognizes the root 'ys 'waste away'.
one recognizes the negative 'i.
From Gk. iaspidi, dat. sg. of iaspis 'jasper'; also iyyāspid (from Geez). See also iyyāspin.
from Gk. iaspin (acc. sg. of iaspis) 'jasper'. See also iyyāspid, iyyāspis.
from Gk. iaspis 'jasper'; also Syr. 'iyyaspa, 'iyyāspid, 'iyyāspin, yāsbis.
see also 'ay(y)āyāt.
'ayyāt (T.M) 'swelling, lump, bruise'.

ayte-nu 'where?, whither?'; ba-ayte 'where?, in what way?, how?'; 'əm-ayte, 'əm-ayte-nu 'whence?, (Gr.233) by what means?, with what motive?'; haba 'ayte 'whither?, to what place?'; 'ayte-ma 'where?, 'ayte-nu-ma 'where?'; 'ayte-ni 'anywhere'; ('i)-ayte-ni 'nowhere'); 'ayte-hi 'anywhere'. composed of 'ay and -te. SEMITIC: Ar. 'ay-na 'where?', Heb. 'ayye 'where?' (lengthened from 'ay), 'ayin, in min 'ayin 'from where?'. Aram. 'e, 'ayye 'where?'. Syr. 'ay-kā. Ug. 'iy 'where is/are?'. Akk. ai 'where?'. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'aya 'where?', Tna. 'ay 'where?' augmented by -t in Te. 'əttəyā, 'ayti, Amh. yāt, Arg. yed, Gur. et.
could it be a qualification of the earth as being even or level according to Tayyā, and to be compared with 'ayaya, below?

ta'ayaya 'be equal, be comparable, be compared, be paired'; ta'ayāyaka (K*) 'resemble one another'; 'ayəyaya (T.Y.M) rendered by Amh. tämälläšä 'return'; however, in view of 'ayaya, tämälläšä 'is an error for tämässäla 'be like, resemble'; 'ayāwī 'equal'; 'ayāy 'equal, associate, likeness, image, appearance'; 'ayuy 'equal, comparable'; 'ayyagay 'equality, likeness, resemblance, analogy, allegory, example, conformity, harmony'; 'ayāyəta 'equality, in like manner, like'; 'ayāyəyā (T) 'equal, resembling; equality, resemblance'; 'ayāyāt, same as 'ayāt; also 'neither moist nor dry (hide)'; mə'ayāy (Lt) 'point of reference'. SEMITIC: Syr. 'wy in 'āwē 'agreeing, in concord', 'et'ewi 'agree, make an alliance' (Dillmann 797, Noeldeke 1910:190, n.2). The suggestion made by Brockelmann 1927:38 to derive G. 'ayāy from Ar. 'āya 'sign' (not existing in Geez) with the adjectival -āy, the meaning being 'mit demselben Zeichen versehen', is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'ayay 'relative, kinsman', Amh. ayaya 'comrades, partners who are equal in age and status'.
is connected with 'ayāt 'wafer (of honey)'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. hazab 'rein', Amh. əzab, zab, Arg. zab, Gur. əzab; passed into Cushitic: Kam. zaba, Qab. izāba.
corrupt from Gk. nezer, transcription of Heb. nezer 'crown'.
in the Aksum inscription 9, 20 (see Littmann 1912:27); to be identified with 'ənza.
from Ar. 'ādā (dy) 'harm, hurt'.
variant of (hzb I), vastahazaba.
undoubtedly for matəzāl, borrowed from Ar. muta-zāl, from 'azala 'remove, set aside, segregate'; also Amh. mə'əzāl 'division in reciting the Psalms'.
SEMİTİC: Ar. 'azaba 'flow'. ETHIOPIC: perhaps Amh. azəba 'dung of bovines which has collected in the pen'. See also 'azäba.

meaning: 'be strong, grow up, prevail, be superior'; 'asta'azzala (Y) 'strengthen'; 'azzāl, (KBT) 'azzāl 'strong, strong man, courageous, brave'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. azzälä 'be strong, powerful' (from Geez).

meaning: 'tie, bind, sew, (T) braid'; 'azzāb (T.Sw. in D 1397) 'braid of silk'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. azzäbä 'tie, braid, plait'.
from Ar. 'asmar.

from Heb. 'ezōb; in the other Semitic languages: Ar. 'azāb, Aram. 'ezōbā; also Ar. zūfā, Syr. zūpā, Akk. zūpu < Aram.-Syriac > Gk. hyssōpos 'hyssop' (see Lokotsch, no. 566). For a bibliography on the plant, see Baumgartner 26. ETHIOPIC: Amh. azab 'hyssop', Te. 'isop. See also hasop.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. azamər, azamär 'a tree the leaves of which are wrapped around the dabbo-bread when it is baked'.
from Amh. azmāra. See also zammara III.

meaning: 'south wind, south wind'; '(nagästa 'azeb, name of the Queen of Saba); 'azebāwi 'southern'
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC: Ar. 'azyab 'south wind, southeast, south', Ar. (Oman) ezyeb 'north wind', Mh. hazieb 'south wind' (see Vollers, ZDMG 49[1895].507, Landberg 1909:1708-9; Noeldeke 1910:62-3). ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. 'azeb 'south-east, south'. Amh. azeb. For the same word expressing different directions, see also samen.
SEMİTİC: Ar. 'udn, Soq. 'idihen, Heb. 'ozen, Aram. 'udnā, Syr. 'ednā, Md. 'udna, Ug. udn, Akk. uzun. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'əzən, Tna. 'əzən, uzūn, Arg. izin, Gur. əzən. See also 'zn, mā'azən.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'azahīt, zahīt, qəzahīt (q < by hypercorrection), 'ashii (from 'azhīt), Amh. azahīt (from Tna), Te. 'əngah (with augmented n) 'quartz', näggäba 'put quartz stones on a tomb' (denomina-tive). The root is common with Egyptian 'izh 'cut with a reaping-hook' (Conti 1978:163; also RSO 48[1973-4].31, but see Rössler, in Leslau 1983:335-6).

meaning: 'angle, corner, border (of garment)'; 'mā'azən 'cardinal points, zone'; '(əbna mā'azənt 'corner stone'); (ra'sa mā'azənt 'head of a corner, corner stone'); 'mə'azūn (Gr.463) 'angular'
origin_explanation: from 'azn 'ear', above; ETHIOPIC: Tna. mā'azən 'angle, corner', Amh. Gur. mazän.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. azha mäzha, same (from Geez).
transcription of Heb. 'āzārā(h) 'barrier, enclosure' (so Dillmann 1406, rather than of Heb. 'āsārā given by Dillmann 1404, under 'ahazrāh).
see also hazurān.

meaning: 'dark blue, dark green'; 'azraqa (MA) 'dye in a blue or in a green color'
origin_explanation: from Ar. 'azraq 'blue' (for its Persian origin, see Eilers 627); in the other Semitic languages: 'azraq 'dark blue', Md. zaruq 'blue (eyes)', šebā(h) zāraqā(h) bō (Hos. 7, 9) translated by Driver (JTS 33[1932].38) 'hoar hairs are grey upon him' (so also Koehler 269, but Baumgartner 273 'sich einschleichen' following J. Blau, VT 5[1955].341).

meaning: 'order, command, rule, exercise dominion, instruct (an inferior), prescribe, make a will, bequeath'

'causative'; 'ta'azzaza, passive; also 'obey, be under someone's authority'; 'ta'azzaza (K*) 'take turns obeying'; 'cause to order each other'; 'asta'azaza (K*) 'cause to obey one another, cause to order each other'; 'azzāzi, act. part. of 'azzaza; also 'testator, secretary'; 'azzūz 'commanded, ordered, obedient'; 'ta'azzāz 'commandment, decree, command, edict, law, ordinance, order, precept, prescription, will, provision'; 'ta'azzāzi 'commanded, ordered, obedient, submissive'; 'ta'azzāzot 'obedience'; '(vi-ta'azzazot 'disobedience'); 'ma'azzaz (Lt) 'preceptor, instructor'
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC: Ar. 'azza 'incite, urge, instigate' (Ambros). ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'azzäzā 'order', Te. 'azzäza, Amh. azzäzä, Arg. ezzäza, Gaf. izzəzā, Gur. azzəzā; passed into Cushitic: Kham. azes, Bil. aded (with alter-nance d:z), Dar. azaz, Kam. azzağğo.
